eps.name: Chrysanthemum Tea with Dried Tangerine Peels

lines.st: Sir, are you saying such a remarkable individual actually existed in the history of Great Yan?
Yes...
Cataloging books, organizing texts, promoting education... if she did so much for all life under Heaven, why does no one remember her, or even know her name?
Sir, can you identify this character? I don't believe I've ever encountered it before.
It's not that no one remembers her. It's simply that her 'true name in one lone word' was never recorded in the history books.
Isn't it a pity? Her pen captured Great Yan's long history and vast expanse, yet not a single page in the historical records bears her name.
Sir, if I were to complete all the missing records, do you think I could reconstruct her image?
Forty years ago...

lines.x: Sir, sir!
It's raining so hard out there. Why not come inside, have a pot of nice, warm tea, and wait out the rain?
No need. This rain won't be stopping anytime soon—I should keep moving.
Haha. Some people want the rain to stop, some hope it carries on.
The umbrella vendor is smiling from ear to ear, but the passersby are downright miserable.
A fascinating thought...
Friend, you should take a moment to rest. I was asked to remind you: the road ahead is rough and uneven. A certain someone worries you might misstep, and get your shoes wet.
Hmm?
Please just follow me, sir. Your friend is already waiting for you inside.
High Minister... it's been a while.
No... These days, you're... the Grand Tutor.
It has been some time indeed, since we bid each other farewell in Yumen.
I expected to meet with the Sui Regulator and the Ministry of War first upon this return to the capital to deliver my report.
That's why I've arranged to meet with you in advance.
His Imperial Majesty... has passed.
When?
Two weeks ago.
I think... if His Majesty had died of natural causes, you would not be coming to see me about it.
Does this mean...?
The man sitting in the shadows holds out his hand. A black weiqi stone rests in his palm.
Sixty years ago, he escaped from that ancient temple and turned himself into one hundred eighty-one black weiqi stones, which scattered across the lands. Their current whereabouts are unknown.
Two weeks ago, the Sui Regulator found this stone in the capital.
What... has he done?
I do not know.
The only thing I'm certain of is that he could not have entered the Forbidden City... someone must have gone out to meet him.
Investigating this person's identity would be... sensitive.
His machinations likely will not involve others.
I shouldn't have come to you today, but when the convict caused all this turmoil before, you were the one who subdued him.
Grandmaster, do you know anything of this?
Not a thing...
I have not seen him, since the incident.
Then I believe you, Grandmaster.
Do you need anything from me?
Do not linger here. Return to Yumen at once.
Ursus is in constant turmoil, and there is a changing of the guard in Yumen. We must not allow morale to be shaken.
I will handle the Sui Regulator and the Ministry of War myself. It is precisely because you are not entangled in this affair that I can entrust you with such a crucial task.
Understood.
I will see the Sui Regulator continue to investigate the weiqi matter in secret.
During this crucial time, you should remain out of sight, Grandmaster. We did not meet here today.
Regarding the True Lung... how is His Highness Yan Wu?
At this moment, he should be—
Yan Wu...
Yan Wu was the one who went to His Imperial Majesty... on the night he died.
Your Highness, twenty-eight members of the traitor's family have been imprisoned.
But after we checked the list, we found there was a single individual missing—an infant just under a month old.
The Imperial Guard searched the Grand Preceptor's mansion thoroughly today, but found no traces of the baby.
A newborn could not have run off by herself.
Someone went through the trouble to whisk her away.
Your Highness need not worry. The Imperial Guard will find her.
Hmph...
Earlier today, the Minister of Justice requested a meeting.
He seeks His Highness's advice on this series of cases.
Handle them... according to the Laws of Yan.
I'll tell him.
Where is my elder brother now?
According to our reports, he left the capital just half an hour ago. Some of the Imperial Guard have defected with him.
The soldiers guarding the city did not dare to stop them, without orders to do so.
Indeed... I never ordered them to keep him in Baizao.
He was unwilling to stay and left everyone behind.
How many Imperial Guardsmen went with him?
Eighteen.
Even after committing such an unforgivable crime, my elder brother can still send the Grand Preceptor to die in his place; he can still command the unwavering loyalty of these eighteen deathsworn.
How many courtiers are still devoted to him? How many still hope that he will retake the throne?
Yet it is Your Highness who now sits on the throne, not him. Therefore, it falls upon Your Highness to ensure Yan's eternal prosperity.
All castes of Yan may afford hesitation and uncertainty... save Your Highness.
It's been so long since I've left this palace... Grand Commandant, tell me——
Has the spring rain yet ceased, in Baizao?
The rain may stop whenever Your Highness desires.
If Your Highness has changed your mind, you yet have time to stop them.
...I'm exhausted.
The Imperial Kitchen has prepared some babao soup and shredded bamboo shoots. If Your Highness desires?
Very well.
There... there...
Don't cry. We're on our way home. We're almost there.
Pick up the pace, please... if you could.
It's raining too hard. And I can't see too well. Couldn't go any faster, at this rate...
How much farther to the next stage station?
At least thirty li...
Brother, just how urgent is your business... that you'd bring your whole family along to travel on a night like this?
Please... just go faster.
Ahhh! Ahhh!! What the——
*Yanese exclamation* What's somebody doing out here in the wilderness at this hour?
The man looks around, but the endless rain blocks his view. It is too dark for him to see anything.
We'll have to get out here.
Sir, I'll give you all the money I have. Take your carriage to the next stage station. If anyone asks, do not tell them you carried passengers with a baby tonight.
I beseech you, good sir.
But.... what... huh?!
Where are you going in this weather?
We'll follow the path through the northern woods.
Ahhhh!!!!!
What is it?
The baby...
The baby is gone...
It's... it's her.
The baby is sleeping soundly in her cradle, oblivious to the mud and blood on her face.
I did everything you asked.
I know their methods, and I know how to answer them. Don't worry. I won't allow any blood to stain you.
Good sir, I have no way to repay your kindness.
No. You need not thank me. I did not do this for you.
She's done so much for me, yet what I do today will neither clear her name nor even begin to avenge her.
I have no family and no affairs to settle after my death. I ask only one favor of you.
Please, take this girl in.
You need not tell her anything. Just let her grow up and live her life in peace.
You know... if she stays here, it will be risky, with a greater possibility that this old case will be reopened.
But isn't that what you want?
We may be powerless to change the situation right now...
But if one day, be it three or four decades later, the truth is revealed...
All the blood shall not have been shed in vain.
Right then. I swear it.

lines.st: Check, please!

lines.x: Wait a minute! I'm busy!
Oh. All you ordered was a bowl of plain noodles. It's on the house!

lines.st: Come on...
I'll be leaving the money on the counter. Bye.

lines.x: Thank you, come again!

lines.st: Eating so little for lunch at that age—what gives?
Too many colors blind the eyes, too many flavors dull the tongue—one must not indulge in the desire for food.
Didn't you just eat the one bowl of plain noodles?
Hmm. This is my fifth.
I'm not like you—I prefer my food light and simple. I never add any seasonings when I eat—no salt, vinegar, chili, scallions, garlic, or spices at all.
When I'm eating, I want to taste the food itself. If the basics are even slightly less than fresh, my taste buds will detect it instantly.
Judging by your mandarin robes... you're a Deputy Minister with the Judicial Review Office, aren't you? Your tongue must be the greatest judge under Heaven.
Haha. You have a way with words.
When next you feel you've been wronged, come find me at the Judicial Review Office. My name is Yu Cheng.
I... won't need to trouble your magisterial greatness with that sort of thing. I am but a humble officer of the Ministry of Rites. I'm not often wronged.
Well, come see me anyway. I'll treat you to a bowl of noodles. What is your name, young man?
I am called Gu Quan.

lines.x: Hey, Jiang! I'm here!
Coming, coming! What'll it be today, Liu?
I'll have the usual. Any specials today?

Well, persimmons are in season. We also got a fresh batch of chestnuts today. You can either eat them raw—nice and crispy, or cooked—sweet and tender. How about stewed beast belly with chestnuts and then some osmanthus-flavored dried persimmon for dessert?
Nice. Alright, I'll try that then.
By the way, that sign we fixed for your restaurant a while back...
Don't worry. I checked with our chef. This meal is on the house.
You've taken care of all our carpentry work the past few years. Treating you to a few meals is the least we can do.
Hahaha. You're too kind!
He's really a great cook, the greatest under Heaven, I'd say. I can't find food this good anywhere else!
That wooden talisman I gave him, I meant it! He was too modest to accept the gift!
Come on! Enough already!
We're just a little old eatery. It's you who keeps us in business. You don't need to flatter us like that.
By the way, where is that young chef of yours? I didn't see him sitting outside and chatting with the guests today.
Don't even bring him up—he's been sulking since early this morning.

There's this famous food critic going around lately, taking advantage of the buzz from the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies, you know? I hear she's visiting all the hidden little eateries in town, rating them one by one in the newspapers.
I know, I know! I hear she published an article reviewing taverns in Baizao. She's even writing a book, says she wants to compile all the best recipes from Baizao, past and present.
She calls herself, 'The Wandering Foodie' or something like that?

She's certainly ambitious—Baizao has countless taverns, and these places have been making their dishes for generations. To write a book about them, she'll need a wealth of knowledge and a stomach big enough for all that food!

Exactly... But I hear the book she's working on, Baizao Cuisine & Food Culture, already earned some praise from the head chefs of the Four Great Restaurants. And none of them have anything bad to say about her reviews.
Rumor has it, she's dropping in here today.
Well, you have nothing to worry about. With your chef's cooking, even the harshest critic will have nothing to criticize, right?
You know his temper. Whether the reviews are good or bad, he can't stand people talking about his food.
(Whisper) He says he doesn't want to get into a cooking competition, but that's just because he's afraid he'll lose.
Alright. That's enough of that!
Haha. The only thing I'm worried about is people discovering this place, so I won't be able to get a table here anymore.
Knock on wood. Oh. Another customer!
Hey, look, the two of you coming right at this hour.
We're right across from the Judicial Review Office. I guess it's kind of become your unofficial cafeteria.
If it wasn't for this restaurant, I would have resigned and moved home ages ago!
Don't say such things. We have a duty! How could you think of resigning?
What's up? Why so serious?
It's the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies, of course! The busiest time of the year for the Office. I haven't had a good night's sleep in a month—who can keep going like this?
The precautions are necessary, though. There are so many tourists here now. If something bad were to happen, it would be a disaster.
No matter how careful we are... there's just too much going on. Something is bound to happen.
Hey. Enough shop talk. Just enjoy the food.
You think we're talking about work because we want to? Ugh!
By the way, did you hear about the Ding Feng Lou case?
What case? If there's something to say, just say it—why all the secrecy?
I'm not trying to be secretive—this case is just downright eerie.
On the 15th of last month, when Ding Feng Lou was busiest, every one of its hundred or so stoves were burning all day and late into the night.
That night, one of the stoves caught fire, and the staff could not put it out. A handful of chefs were injured.
The Originium pipe sprung a leak, was it? That's nothing new.
Well, the investigators could not determine whether the pipe had been tampered with or not.
Hundreds of people have access to that kitchen. Who knows if someone did something?
You must know that Ding Feng Lou is the most famous restaurant in Baizao, and the organizer of the Feast.

The thing that makes this incident particularly strange is that it happened on the 15th day of the month, and the fire started on the hundredth stove. Some say they saw a strange silhouette amidst the flames.
Let's stick to the facts, yes? We don't need to fabricate things here.
I'm telling you the truth! Many people said they saw it!
The work you do at the Judicial Review Office is pretty interesting. Listening to you talk about your cases is like reading a detective novel.
Come now. We're hardly authors here. It's just that whenever a case drags on for a few weeks without progress, I have nightmares about it.
Actually, now that you mention it, I also heard about a strange case, recently.
There's nothing supernatural about it. It's just plain weird.
Oh? Another juicy story? Do tell!
I really shouldn't divulge too many details about this.
You can't tease us like that! The suspense is killing me!
This is a matter of great importance. The place where this incident occurred is truly significant!
It was the Mr. Ning's manor, former Minister of Rites.
My shifu has been investigating this case for a while now. I've only heard an overview. He won't tell me more.
Mr. Ning has been retired for several years now. He's in the process of leaving Baizao and moving back to his hometown in Jiangnan. But a few days ago, a strange thief broke into his mansion.
Nothing was stolen, and no one was hurt. But in one of the guest rooms, there was a painting of a Xian hanging on the wall, and someone used melted honey to draw a line across the Xian's mouth.
Of course, the mansion was heavily guarded, but the thief seemed to know the layout very well.
And that thief—whether down to incredible skill or sheer experience—left no trace behind. My shifu and the others have their hands full trying to crack the case.

Sealing the lips with honey? Isn't that from the 'Legend of the Stove God'? It ensures the Stove God will 'say more good things than bad'. The thief vandalized a painting, but didn't steal anything of value. They don't care for money, so did they have some other motive?
What motive could they have had? Minister Ning is an honorable mandarin. He has a decades-long record of dutiful public service. Who could have any issue with him?
Regardless, he is an important court mandarin. The ransacking of his manor is no small matter, especially at a time like this.
It's our greatest fear, come true.
Oh! Hey! I just thought of something!
You scared me! What's the thought?
I just remembered that I also saw something weird a few days ago, right in this very restaurant!
It was three days ago, I was here eating when a real strange customer walked in.
He looked to be on the older side, and he was still wearing a prison outfit, as if he had just been released by the Judicial Review Office.
He sat down and ordered twenty bowls of white rice, nothing else. He downed them in no time flat!
That's... all you're going to eat?
Eating all that rice is bound to make you choke. Why don't we whip up two little entrees for you, and a bowl of soup. On the house.
No.
It's Dahuang rice, nice and fresh, the finest on the land. What other food could I possibly need?
I've filled my belly. I've no need for any free dishes. Check, please!

After the funny fella finished eating, he grabbed all twenty empty bowls and walked out of the restaurant. He kept walking until he reached the Judicial Review Office. He threw the pile of bowls at the door of the office, and yelled—
'I'm the one who set the fire in the scrollery!'
Well, I'm sure you know more about it than me, especially with my terrible memory. Who was that guy anyway? What happened to him in the end?
There's no way I could discuss something like that with you...
Shhh! There are civilians at the next table over!
Don't worry about them. I heard that lady speaking Victorian. I think they're foreigners. They don't understand what we're saying anyway.
(In perfect Yanese) Laoban, diancan!
'Red Silk Special,' 'Mala Tofu,' 'Soft Yellow Shells,' 'Autumn Casserole,' and 'Jade Surprise'...
...and that's all she ordered.
Is she trying to challenge us?
She seems very polite. She just looked at the menu and picked out these dishes. Didn't look like she was asking for trouble.
You think she might be that food critic, the one who wrote 'Baizao Cuisine & Food Culture'?
What kind of food critic comes in and orders nothing but tofu?!
Relax, chef, relax.
You know how things are going here, business-wise. The future of our restaurant hinges upon this very moment!
You have to do your best. The dishes must be flawless!
The Victorian woman pulls up a chair and two jugs of wine and sits at the table with the two mandarins of the Judicial Review Office.
She casually pours each a glass of wine; its rich, sweet aroma soon fills the table.
Please, drink and enjoy! This wine was meant for sharing!
Aiai, so what happened next? What became of the weirdo who threw the empty rice bowls?
Good lady, what you just heard was nothing more than idle diner's chatter.
Whatever you do, please do not share it with anyone else.
Huh? But you haven't finished the story yet. Where's the cliffhanger? Where's the 'stay tuned for the next episode'?
I am a mandarin of the Judicial Review Office, madam. I'm not a storyteller in the teahouse.
What a coincidence! I have a good friend who works there too.
And here I was, thinking everybody at the Judicial Review Office was stuffy, pretentious, and tight-lipped.
*cough* *cough* *ahem*...
Dear madam, have you come to Yan all by yourself? Are you here on vacation?
Let's not be so formal. My Yanese name is Huang, or Blaze in Victorian.
And yes... I guess you could say I'm here on vacation.
I haven't met many Victorians who speak Yanese as well as you do. Have you lived in Yan previously?
Haha. Actually, no. But I'm not a hundred percent Victorian. My father is Yanes—
Three rules, you say?
Yes, if you can't follow these rules, I won't be able to take you back to Baizao.
Okay, okay. Lay 'em on me.
First, be wary of strangers. Do not, of your own volition, take actions that are unnecessary.
What do you take me for? A three-year-old? I know that. What else?
Second, do not tell anyone that you are an Infected.
Do I really need you to tell me that?
Third...
Do not, under any circumstances, tell anyone about your father.
...Okay.
Blaze, you are an important witness in all of these major cases… as well as an important piece of evidence.
I need your cooperation, and in return, I will guarantee your safety.
What's that about your dad?
Errr... I'm here to visit some family... and I'm on vacation. Haha.

Well, madam, you came at the perfect time. The Feast of a Hundred Delicacies is about to begin! All Baizao's restaurants compete to serve the best dishes. This is the time of year when you can try the tastiest food!
Really? Do you have any Baizao delicacies you can recommend, or maybe a restaurant or two?
Baizao doesn't have any signature dishes, or 'Baizao delicacies', as you put it. Instead, you'll find that every single dish you eat in Baizao makes a delicacy.
Ah, that makes sense! I didn't make the connection until now that Baizao means hundred stoves in Victorian!
You know what they say, 'To the people, food is heaven.' Yes, the name 'Baizao', bestowed ages ago by the reigning True Lung, means to tend all cooking stoves under heaven.
Later, someone says 'Exquisite food demands meticulous preparation.' See, fine food doesn't just fill your stomach, it soothes your soul.
There are different dishes for every season and every region. The best chefs? They have a wealth of knowledge, and the power of the gods, at their fingertips.
Please. Let's eat. Enough fooling around with foreign tourists.
Order up!
Do you have some special fondness for tofu, or...?
Hmm? These are all tofu dishes? They have different names, so I had no idea.
Who would have thought that Yan had so many different kinds of tofu?
Whatever. There's more than enough tofu for everyone. We'll enjoy it all together. Isn't that the custom here in Yan, after all?
Alright, let's dig in!
What's this? I don't remember ordering a dessert...
Hmm?
Did you send out the food? Does she like it?
Actually, she didn't really eat much. She gave most of it away to the other customers.
Ugh...
Settle down. She might be feeling out the ambiance, you know.
Just calm down. Let's see what happens.
So what is this 'Feast of a Hundred Delicacies' you keep talking about?
You may speak fluent Yanese, madam, but there are clearly gaps in your knowledge of our culture.
The Feast of a Hundred Delicacies happens every year in Mid-Autumn, when the True Lung convenes meetings with mandarins at all levels to discuss state affairs.
If it's just a bunch of government meetings. Why is it called the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies?
Now, that has a beautiful origin story.
Many years ago, the True Lung at the time held a birthday banquet, and all the mandarins presented him with gifts, including all manner of rare treasures from all across Yan.
But there was one mandarin, a young Assistant Minister from the Ministry of Revenue, who gave the True Lung a bowl of 'Pearl Jade Soup'.
The True Lung lifted the lid off the bowl, only to find a clump of rotten cabbage leaves and a scant few grains of brown rice floating in some water.

The True Lung was enraged, demanding, 'What is the meaning of this?!' The Assistant Minister replied, 'There was a flood in Jiangqi this year, so we had a terrible harvest. What you see in the bowl here is what the peasants have been eating every day.'
Speaking up for what's right—that's what any good mandarin should do.
Still, doing it to the True Lung's face, on his birthday like that, takes some serious guts.
Yes. The surprising thing is, the True Lung did not punish the young mandarin—he praised him and ordered that all other mandarins follow his example in advocating for the people.
The True Lung ordered cuts to the court budget and used those funds for disaster relief. He built embankments to guard against future floods, and Jiangqi was saved.
Yes, and the following year, the mandarins presented the True Lung with a bowl of sumptuous 'Fortune Soup' to show him that the finneries were thriving.
*ahem*...
In summation, the annual banquet became a tradition, called the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies, held every year during the Mid-Autumn Festival.
And all restaurants in the city serve dishes from the traditional Hundred Delicacies menu, welcoming all the people to join in the celebration.
Oh. The Yanese really know food, don't they?
This little shop here is a great example. It may look ordinary, but it serves top notch eats! Just look how many different types of tofu they can make! Amazing!
Then again, these cakes here... they taste different to the ones I've had before, and not in a good way. I think something's not quite right with them.
Huh? Why's everyone gone silent?
Was it you?!
You're the one criticizing my food?!
What do you want to show me?
Pick up the pace a little, we'll be there soon.
If you walk in the snow by the stream, the fragrant plum blossoms are rare. However, you can find some at the base of the mountain.
I suppose the view of the fireworks against the city skyline from atop the mountain could be the only thing worth climbing all the way up here.
Fireworks? Who cares about those?! I brought you here to see... this pine tree.
An evergreen... It can withstand the cold weather.
No. That's not what's special about it.
I planted this tree myself, right before I assumed my post in Baizao. Back then, I had the same mindset as you.
After all these years, I'm a different person now, and this tree is a different tree.
I do not understand.
That doesn't matter. All you need to do now is look at the tree; imprint it in your memory.
Qingyan, you will do well in the Judicial Review Office. You will go on to be an excellent Deputy Minister or maybe a minister yourself!
Those cases will not be a problem for you. There's really only one issue that you'll need to focus on—
Will you be able to handle the truth?
I do not understand.
You don't need to understand now. Every once in a while, you will think back to this pine tree. Once you've spent enough time contemplating the tree, you will understand the rest.
After I die, cut down the tree. Use its wood to fashion my coffin.
Mr. Yu...
We should be going. The higher you climb, the colder it gets.

lines.st: Years of wind and snow, moments fleeting like blossoms in the morning and evening light.
Once more, I think back to the pine tree and the mountain path, buried in snow.
Yu Cheng, what penalty do you propose to atone for your guilt?

lines.x: (snoring softly)
*cough* *cough*
Huh? What time is it? Has the suspect confessed yet?
Censor Jie Zhen, this is a serious matter. Please mind your words and behavior.
This is a routine internal matter for the Judicial Review Office. The Department of Discipline and Supervision is just going through the motions. It's not a big deal.
However, this trial today certainly has been interesting. The minister is usually quite well-tempered. I've never seen her that angry before.
Yu Cheng, you served in the Judicial Review Office once. You took an oath before the bronze mirror.
Do you really think the Judicial Review Office is the proper place for you to act like a spoiled brat?!
That's an unjust accusation. You asked me all that you needed to ask, and I answered them. What else would you have me do?
On the fifteenth of last month, around 1:45 AM, someone set fire to the southern end of the Judicial Review Office scrollery. That person was me.
Liar! When the fire broke out, you were in pre-trial detention. How could you have started it?
If you planned the fire, you had help. Name your co-conspirator.
I had no co-conspirator. However, I did have a helper.
A fowlbeast.
That night, a wounded fowlbeast landed by the window of my cell. I fed it a few grains of rice and some fresh water.
The fowlbeast was quite intelligent. I saved it, and it was willing to return the favor. I gave it a burning straw and told it to fly to the scrollery.
Whaa???
*cough* *cough*
Yu Cheng, the courtroom has no time for your games!
I'm not playing games. Ask me anything, and I will answer truthfully.
Very well. You say you started the fire. Elaborate on that, if you will.
What species of fowlbeast was it that you used?
Do I look like a fowlbeast expert? I only remember that it was completely black, without a single stray hair... as black as the human heart.
You said you gave the fowlbeast a burning straw. You were locked in a cell. Where did you get the straw?
I picked it out of my sleeping cushion.
Your cushion was made of cotton.
Then my memory is mistaken. It must have fallen in from the fowlbeast's nest.
The guards did a full search of your cell after the fire. They did not find any fowlbeast nest.
If criminals didn't try to cover their tracks, we wouldn't need the Judicial Review Office or the Ministry of Justice, would we?
That fowlbeast truly understood human nature.
Why you...

Oh, right, as for how I lit the straw in the first place—I struck the cell bars with these iron shackles exactly eight hundred sixty-three times, one for each year of the Judicial Review Office's existence.
That is as much detail as I can provide on the fire. Will it do?
Madam Minister, do you have more questions for me?
It seems that he has been reading some fanciful stories since his retirement.
This is no time for jokes.
Well, she had the patience to play along with him and indulge his insanity.
Do you want to enter this line into the record?
...Of course.
Interesting...
Yu Cheng was once appointed Deputy Minister for the Judicial Review Office's Haoran Pavilion. There, he took down countless corrupt mandarins and notorious criminals.
However, after his retirement, you convicted him of fabricating evidence, which is how he ended up here.
As you think back now, do you regret your choice, Deputy Minister Lin?
The law is the law. It bends for no man. Stop trying to stir up trouble, Censor Jie.
But when we look at it, Yu Cheng's behavior is quite odd. When he was charged with fabricating evidence, he did not question it or protest. Now, after he's been convicted, he decides to start a fire?
That's not right...
I still have questions!
Yes, Deputy Minister Lin?
...Hmph.
Lin Qingyan puts down her pen and paper and happens to meet Yu Cheng's gaze. The prisoner, who has been pretending to be insane, is now staring at her intently.
She wants to know the truth. She does not fear revealing herself in the process.
Yu Cheng seems pleased with Lin Qingyan's reaction. He closes his eyes, as an ugly grin crosses his face.
Deputy Minister Lin, as acting clerk, you need only take notes.
Minister Chen, there is one question you haven't yet asked. Yu Cheng claims he set the fire. What was his motive?
The suspect's confession is clearly nonsense. Why bother asking his motive?
Hahaha. I am the one who set fire to the scrollery. You must believe me.
As for why I did it...
Yu Cheng!

There were seventy-four thousand case files in the southern section of the scrollery, the result of sixty years of painstaking effort and the hard work of twenty-four deputy ministers—and you saw fit to turn it all to ash?
Yu Cheng suddenly droops his head. He remains silent for what seems like ages. The Minister's eyes remain fixed on him, watching his every move.
As I see it, it was no great loss.
Those files were filled with half-truths, whitewashed fabrications, and outright lies. Gazing upon them dirties the eyes; thinking of them taints the mind.
So I thought it best to cleanse it all in flames.
Cleanse?
Just what are you trying to say?
What am I trying to say? If you keen to know, then let's have a discussion: that which I burned, was it clean, or dirty?
Minister Chen, The suspect is clearly suffering from some sort of mental disease. It's getting late. Shall we conclude the questioning for today?
Escort Yu Cheng back to his cell.
I truly regret that we subjected the Department of Discipline and Supervision to this circus here today.
It is truly a vexing case. We understand.
The Judicial Review Office will be sure to submit a full explanation to the Department of Discipline and Supervision.
Sigh... I just hope this can be resolved quickly, so I don't have to make so many trips here. You've planted way too many camphor trees in this courtyard—they're making me sneeze.
I shall take my leave now, Deputy Minister Lin. See you.
You should be off too, Lin Qingyan.
The courtroom door swings open, as sunlight pours in once more. Lin Qingyan immediately spots the seven towers with eighteen-pointed roofs hidden in the shadows of the trees.
The Judicial Review Office scrollery were all built in this style. The roofs of the towers seem to be covered with ash, the remnants of the burned southern section.
Just as she is about to step out the door, she hears a faint voice.
Lin Qingyan, it's almost time.
Do not forget.
Lin Qingyan steps out into the cold sunlight without even hesitating.
Achoo!
Censor Jie Zhen, you're still here?
I was waiting to chat with you for a minute. It's not convenient to talk in there.
If you still want save... I mean... investigate Yu Cheng's case, you'd better move quickly.
After the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies is over, all prisoners detained in Baizao will either be transferred to another prison or exiled. In any case, the cells must be emptied within two weeks.
Two weeks...
The imperial decree should reach you shortly. I just spotted it early, so I'm giving you a heads-up.
And here I thought you didn't care much for these things.
It is within my power to avoid caring, but you cannot get it out of your head. Yu Cheng is very important to you, isn't he?
He was kind of like my teacher in the Judicial Review Office. He's the one who taught me to distinguish right from wrong.
When I first discovered that he had fabricated evidence to convict a corrupt mandarin... well, I shrugged it off as 'using any means necessary to achieve justice.'
However, I never imagined he would set fire to the scrollery. There's absolutely no justice in that.
Indeed... I can't figure him out.
But it does provoke a question... They've never emptied the jails for the Feast before. Why do it this year?
This matter may not concern you directly, but I'm sure you've heard something about it?
Yumen experienced a Catastrophe at the beginning of the year. Then, this summer, we suffered the Dahuang incident.
And what was the root cause of all this?
...The bestial Sui.
This thousand-year-old case must finally be resolved...
I dare not engage in presumptuous speculation. This is as much as I can say for now.
Duoxie...
Censor Jie Zhen... why did you terminate the ongoing interrogation?
Ah...
As the saying goes, 'Words may deceive, so trust your eyes.'
I wager you haven't seen the burned scrollery yet.
No, I have not.
Perhaps you'd better go see for yourself.
I will take my leave.
The restaurant, which was loud and boisterous just a moment ago, goes completely silent.
The regulars, anticipating what is to come, file out of the diner one-by-one. Of course, they remember to leave money for their meals.
Jiang, close the door.
...*sigh*
What? What happened?
Jiang, wasn't she just eating these tangerine cakes here?
Yes, she was.
And didn't you tell me she was criticizing the taste?
No. She said they taste 'different', not bad, just 'different'.
(winking vigorously)
I guess you'd call it criticism. They're not exactly orthodox, different to what I had before.
So you're the head chef, eh? Pretty young, aren't you?
Don't try to change the subject!
I was just critiquing the food. That's not a capital offense!
Besides, I thought the rest of the dishes were awesome.
Now you're on to flattery! This is my restaurant, and I have my rules! You either tell me what's wrong with the tangerine cakes, or I won't let you leave!
I told you already. They taste different to the ones I've had before.
Where, and when, did you eat them before?
Oh maybe... more than ten years ago? When I was living in Victoria, a relative sent them to me.
Did you come here to stir up trouble? Jiang! Escort her out!
No. We're not in the habit of kicking customers out of the restaurant...
Good madam, think for a minute. What exactly is it about these cakes that tastes different? Maybe this is all some kind of misunderstanding?
Let me think... I remember the tangerine cakes I had before were sweeter. Yes, you must not have used enough sugar.
The tangerine cakes need more sugar? Hahaha...
To think I took you for a food critic. You are a woman with absolutely no taste. I won't waste my time arguing with you. Please leave.
You say I have no taste, when the real problem is your cooking?
Oh... no...
My cooking, you say?
Don't make me laugh! My cooking... my culinary talent, my—
Alright then. I'll make you a bet. Go out this door here and sample the food at some of the other restaurants. If you find a better tasting meal, I'll give you this entire shop!
(softly) No, please no.
But if you can't find better food, you have to sit at this table by the window and shout out to the streets...
Shout what?
Apologize to me! One hundred times!
But what would I want with your restaurant?

Let's do this instead. If I find food that tastes better, then you'll let me eat here for a month, free, and you'll teach me how to cook a few of the tastiest dishes in Yanese cuisine, also free of charge.
Huh?
There's an old Yanese saying, 'Give a man a fin and you'll feed him for a day. Teach a man to fincatch, and you'll feed him for a lifetime.'
This restaurant... when I leave, I can't take it with me. If you teach me how to cook a few tasty dishes, I can cook them for my friends.
But weren't you just criticizing my cooking?
I told you, all your other dishes taste great.
Wait. Wait a moment! Don't think you can just trick me like that! We still need to discuss the tangerine cakes!
Okay. Then I'll take your bet. I know I can find tangerine cakes, somewhere in Baizao, that taste like the ones I had before.
Hmph...
Huh? What's that weird smell?
I think it's coming from back in the kitchen...
I know that smell. It's like something is overcooked.
My stew!
Dear customer, I apologize. Our head chef has a bad temper and is easily offended.
Please come again, alright?
Pretty weird...
I've heard that skilled chefs tend to be eccentric.
In fact, I'll bet the reason he was able to become a good chef was precisely because he takes his food so seriously.
Did you notice that each of the five tofu dishes you ordered had tofu cut in a different way, yet every piece of tofu within each dish was exactly the same size and shape?
I don't think many chefs in Baizao could accomplish that.
Hm? You overheard our argument?
Sorry, I wasn't eavesdropping. You were just within earshot.
You were all having such fun, sharing your dishes amongst yourselves. Well, I believe you accidentally took the plate of tangerine cakes that I ordered.
Oh! Sorry about that!
What a dummy I was, taking someone else's food and then having a fight with the chef over it.
You wait here. I'll order you some more, straight away!
No. There's no need for that.
I came here today to try the food. After seeing how much you all enjoyed it, well, I figure it must be good enough.
However, I'm still a little curious, what exactly did you not like about the tangerine cakes?
I can't really put my finger on it, but I just know I've eaten better ones before.
Are you sure the dish you ate before was indeed 'tangerine cakes'?
Of course!
One of my relatives lived in Baizao, and would ship me these cakes. There's no way I'd be wrong... I'm sure.
May I trouble you with a request, then?
You're going out looking for another restaurant that serves these tangerine cakes, right? Can you take me with you?
Sure! You're from Baizao, aren't you? That's perfect. You know your way around these parts!
Then again, it's getting kind of late. Let's meet here again tomorrow at noon. What do you say?
Certainly.
Oh, right. By the way, my name is Blaze. I'm a tourist from Victoria. How should I call you?
I am a woman of few hobbies. I like to write, and I like to eat.
My pen name is 'The Wandering Foodie'. In any case, you may call me Xingzhu for short.
Such a trove of knowledge, those wonderful archives...
Ashes to ashes... dust to dust...
The dark space still has a lingering, acrid smell. On the collapsed beams of ironwood, murky raindrops tremble, ready to fall.
The iron chests that once held so many records and books have been warped and twisted by the flames. The paper and ink within have smoldered into ash, leaving only faint traces of words.
Do not linger too long. Rain showers after a fire send smoke up into the air.
What have we learned so far?
We've determined this to be arson.
The absurd part? The fire's time and location match Yu Cheng's confession perfectly.
The scrollery was heavily guarded, yet on the night of the fire, the guards didn't notice anything.
Not only was the perpetrator highly skilled in kung fu, but they also had intimate knowledge of the Judicial Review Office's layout. They must have been working with Yu Cheng for quite some time.
You're saying... you think this was an inside job?
We cannot be certain.
How bad was the damage?
All records that were burned were from old cases, which has little effect on our current work. However, if these cases were ever to be reviewed in the future, there would be no evidence to go on.
Nearly all of these cases were Yu Cheng's responsibility.
Do you really believe Yu Cheng was the one who burned the scrollery?
Absolutely not...
No matter what nonsense he spouts, he was imprisoned when the fire broke out. There's no way he could have done it himself.
However, I'm sure he's involved in some way. He's working with someone else.
What did he mean when he suggested the files are 'dirty'?
Is there something on your mind?
Do you still remember... the last case Yu Cheng investigated before he retired?
How could I forget?
Forty years ago, the Minister of Justice was stabbed in the street. The assassin turned out to be a mad beggar.
The Judicial Review Office did everything in their power to uncover the man's identity, to no avail.
In the end, they classified the murder as a random, spur-of-the moment act of violence.
But nearly thirty years later, Yu Cheng accused the sitting Minister of Rites, Ning Shu, of being the murderer.
I worked with Yu Cheng for many, many years. I knew him better than anyone.
However, in his last few years at the Office, he began to change.
Something radicalized him. He began doing 'whatever it takes' to catch criminals. He even started fabricating evidence to close cold cases and settle old scores.
If the accusations against him were baseless, then why did Minister Ning resign?
What do you mean?
Do you still remember... Gu Quan?
It all began when Gu Quan, serving as the envoy of ceremonial rites, died en route to Victoria...
Yu Cheng believed that Minister Ning murdered the Minister of Justice, and then killed the important witness in the case thirty years later.
But that's the absurdity... the envoy had only just taken office. How could he have been connected to a case from thirty years prior?

Moreover, the envoy's manner, place, and time of death were all clearly established: he drowned near the Victorian border. How could the Minister of Rites have orchestrated his death, all the way from Baizao?
The Judicial Review Office recorded Gu Quan's death in the case files...
The files are somewhere in this pile of ash that lays before you...
Why raise these two cases now? Do you think they are connected to the fire somehow?
I would call it a hunch...
Yu Cheng's confession has drawn our attention, but clearly, there's some kind of larger scheme behind the fire. The real question isn't who set the fire, but the arsonist's ultimate goal.
Would you hand this case over to me?
No.
On what grounds?!
Yu Cheng asked you to take notes during his trial. This is why you stand here now, and it is also the reason I cannot allow you to participate any further.
Whatever Yu Cheng's plan, you are most certainly a part of it.
I know you regard him as a mentor and couldn't bear to see his reputation tarnished, but I will assign someone else to investigate the fire. Rest assured, we will get to the bottom of this.
What about the cases that Yu Cheng once investigated?
You'd best forget about those.
No matter what you intend to investigate, it's simply not a good time.
Why do you say that?
Lin Qingyan, you are a Deputy Minister in the Judicial Review Office, not some patrolwoman. Every case you handle has a ripple effect.
Your so-called 'act of justice' may very well become a dagger in the hands of those with ulterior motives.
So you know what's really going on with these cases, don't you?
Stay away from Yu Cheng's cases. That is my suggestion. Do not force me to make it an order.
Oh, and I have one final piece of advice...
You must learn to prioritize. The past is never as important as the present.
The young Deputy Minister is still standing in the same spot.
She walks down the path that's been cleared through the rubble, through the place that once held so many case files.
The arsonist's motive is quite clear: he never wanted those files to see the light of day.
Whether Yu Cheng fabricated evidence or not, all available clues in both cases point to Ning Shu.
But why would the Minister of Rites assassinate another high-ranking mandarin?
She bends down and brushes aside a charred beam, revealing a hint of green emerging from the ashes.
It's a pine tree.
Yu Cheng, you...
I planted this tree myself, right before I assumed my post in Baizao.
After I die, cut down the tree. Use its wood to fashion my coffin.
Lin Qingyan, it's almost time.
Do not forget.
This month's water bill is two thousand, and electricity is two thousand five hundred, while ingredient costs...
Go do your accounting somewhere else please. I can't stand you buzzing in my ear.
According to my calculations, if you can come up with another three thousand, we'll be able to keep the doors open past this month.
And where am I going to dig out that coin?

There are ways to do it—like the two months of meals owed by Wang the carpenter, or you can visit Shopkeeper Liu across the street and ask him for the balance he owes for last month's funeral banquet.
Collect enough of those outstanding debts, and we'll have what we need to make it through the month.
That's... less than ideal... isn't it?
Customers on credit are in a tough spot. Going up to them and calling in the debt right now... feels awkward, to say the least.
You want to play the good guy? Why, every time the rent collector comes calling, do you ask me to deal with him?
You're... big-boned. Even if the rent-collector is angry, he won't take you in a fight.
Maybe... there's another way...
Ding Feng Lou is coming here again today to hand out invitations to the competition.
They're hiring chefs in preparation for the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies. If you can manage to win the competition, the prize money alone will be enough to pay our rent through the end of the year!
No! No way, no how!
I am the proud owner and head chef of 'Savors at Yu's'! How could I stoop so low as to work for someone else?

This is no ordinary cook! The head chef of Ding Feng Lou doesn't take apprentices, but even a casual demonstration provides enough to keep most chefs learning for three to five years. Plenty of people dream of working under him, never to get the chance!
Besides, it's for the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies! The menu is recorded every year and passed down through generations. This is the lifelong dream of countless chefs—
Those who want to attend are free to go. I shall not.
Are you scared?
I'm not scared! You're scared!
Hmmph. What Feast of a Hundred Delicacies? It's nothing more than a group of old gray-hairs hosting a big dinner party.
All they ever talk is politics. They don't even have a chance to eat. It's a waste of time.
Just listen to your arrogance. You talk as if you've been there in person.
I've catered so many banquets. They're all the same.
You won't take any of my suggestions! I guess we're just going to let this restaurant fail then!
Good grief, with a boss like you, I've got to start making new career plans.
Okay, okay. If it's just another three thousand we need, I'll start making extra deliveries each day through the end of the month.
Have you no ambition? As a chef, don't you want your restaurant to do well? I'm not even sure why you opened it in the first place.
People need to eat, which means there has to be someone to cook for them. As long as we have customers who want to eat my food, this restaurant will do just fine.
More importantly, I promised I'd wait for a certain customer, and that one still haven't shown. I can't close the shop yet.
What customer? Who do you need to wait for?
It's just not time yet. A great stew demands patience. You have to add in the ingredients at just the right time.
There's no need to rush.
What's with this thing? I can't get any channels. I want to watch the news!
Hey. You're back.
Indeed.
What have you learned?
I brought some food for you. I found a new restaurant today. The kitchen stinks to high heaven, but the cooking is pretty solid.
Oh, right, there's one other thing. Today I found these snacks that my dad once sent me a long time ago.
Who gave you permission to leave you room as you please?
Wha?
Seriously? I've been living off takeout for three days straight. I just wanted to go out and grab a bite to eat—is that not allowed?
How many times have I told you? Your identity and status are extremely important; your current situation is dreadfully tenuous. Are you taking this seriously or not?
Lin Qingyan! Are you off your rocker? We already established the three rules. No one said I was confined to my room!
I had a good life back at Rhodes Island. Then you showed up and told me my father's death was suspicious, without giving any details.

I came all the way here to Baizao, the place where my dad used to live, and you still haven't told me anything! You've made me stay in this room, day after day, and now you have the gall to yell at me?
I... that is—
Forgive me. I should not have scolded you. The current situation is not as I had anticipated.
What 'situation'? Is there anything I can do to help?
I don't know. A lot of unexpected things happened in Baizao before we arrived.
If you would aid me, then simply do what you can to avoid danger, understood?
As for your father, I just happened to be handed that case a few years ago.
The case was originally being handled by my predecessor, someone I trusted deeply. Your father seemed to have been spending a lot of time pursuing a certain 'truth' in the last few years of his life.
His death was suspicious. Every clue we've found hints that there were larger forces at work there. How large? We don't know for certain, nor can we even hope to guess.
But your mere existence is the most important piece of evidence we have.
Blaze, I vow to you I will find the truth, and once I do, I will tell you everything.
However, until I do, I simply need you to trust me. I need you to cooperate with me.
By the way, what were you saying about your father just now?
I said I found these snacks that he used to send to me a long time ago.
And?
They don't taste the same as they used to. Maybe it's just because I'm remembering wrong.
The Yanese have a saying for this, right? 'The situation remains the same, yet the people have changed.'
No. That's not right. The situation has also changed.
Well then, a more apt saying would be, 'Times change, and with them, change all things.'
Too much time has passed. It will be very hard for us to find any clues left from back then.
But these are in fact one of the few things I've found in Baizao that relates to my father.
When I lived with my mother in Victoria, my dad would send them to me from time to time.
I figured that by the time I got my disease under control and figured out what I really believed in, my dad would be back in Victoria, waiting for me to come home. Naive.
Everyone longs for that sort of 'happy ending'.
And yet, by the time I found a place that could treat my Oripathy and finished some of the things I had to do with the bunny's crew... my dad... was gone.
Before you came and found me, I always thought my dad's death was an accident. I was sad, but there was nothing I could do about it.
But if you're telling me my father was murdered.... then that's an entirely different story.
I need to learn the truth. I owe that to my father... and myself.
In all truth, I know very little about what happened to your father. Many of the old archives have been lost, and very little information can be extracted from what remains.

Just from looking at the case files he left behind, we can see that your father worked as a ceremonial officer in the Ministry of Rites for over ten years but never got promoted. It seems he was not well-suited to serve in public office.
However, to stay in a position like that for over a decade... he must have been a good person.
Good people... good people also die.
She walks over to the window, closes her eyes, and rubs her temples.
The faraway city lights are shimmering in the twilight. The years of wandering are long over.
People today are moving from one stage of life to the next, as the city guides them and runs right behind them.
Many boundaries are blurred, many high walls become thoroughfares, many hurried footsteps pass over the paving stones. Not everyone is qualified to etch their name into the annals of history.
It's high autumn.

The courtyard is silent. Its long, thick walls prevent outside noise from coming in. It's empty, save for an old persimmon tree leaning against the wall in the shade, with its old crown extending over the top of the wall.
No one knows what this old tree has witnessed over the years. We do know that, since its planting, it has turned red, and green, and red again, as the seasons have flown by.
Each year, bright red, fresh persimmons grow from its branches, and each year, these beautiful fruits wipe away old memories of previous harvests.
The old man sits under the tree, sleeping in the shade.
...Grandfather.
Grandfather?
Huh.... wha?
Ciqiu? You're back?
Yes. I got delayed a bit on the way back, but thankfully, I made it in time for the Mid-Autumn festival.

I've been thinking a lot these past few days. I've been watching the persimmons grow redder and redder. I was afraid that if you didn't come back soon, we wouldn't be able to eat them together this year.
I'm not sure how many more harvests I will live to see. If you had come back any later, you may not have even seen me here...
Grandfather...
Ning Ciqiu decides not to crack any jokes, as she usually would have done. She looks at the old man closely. He seems to have a few new wrinkles on his forehead this time.
The autumn wind blows suddenly, ruffling the old man's hair.
We completed our trial of Dahuang's first mobile plate last month. I've finished up my work at the Ministry of Rites for now, so they allowed me two weeks' vacation.
You've worked really hard.
I heard about the goings-on in Dahuang as soon as they happened. I've detailed all my criticisms in my letters.

I know you were involved in the disaster relief effort as well, but every Yanese mandarin is duty-bound to protect the people. As an Assistant Minister of the Ministry of Rites, you must take responsibility for the poor management here.
Deducting half a year's pay is letting you off easy. You have nothing to complain about.
Of course I shall not complain, and I take my errors to heart.
Ciqiu, you must remember: do not focus on what you've already done; focus on what you still haven't done yet.
As a magistrate, you must carry out the orders of your superiors, while winning over and maintaining the trust of those below you, the citizens.

In your position, everything you do is only what was expected of you in the first place. However, should you fail in even the slightest of your responsibilities, it would be an unforgivable transgression.
If you do not understand this, you are not fit to wear your magisterial robes.
'Devote all your energy to the cause, as that is all you have to give.' I still remember the words you taught me so many moons ago.
I shall take this mistake to heart, but I shan't despair. Quitting now would really be shirking my responsibilities.
Yes.
Okay. I've said enough on this for now. I am not in your position, nor do I work in the Ministry of Rites. Therefore, I have no right to interfere.
Now, I am more worried about your injury. Are you well?
Do not worry, Grandfather. I recovered quite some time ago.

Broken bones take at least three months to heal, so please take caution. In a few days, after you get settled, make the time to go see a doctor. An old friend of mine gave me medicine patches. I'll give you some to take back home with you.
Very well, Grandfather.
You kids are always making me worry.
I'm just about to move back to my old hometown. My son and daughter never come to see me, and your sister is never home. Everyone's forgotten me.
What you say about my sister sounds true, but as far as you being forgotten... forgive me, but these past few days you've closed your door to all visitors.
Ugh. This again? Kids these days, you don't have your priorities straight, do you?
I haven't had a single moment of peace in this courtyard since the beginning of last month.

The youth today are clever, dare not knock on my door to offer me wine or tea. Instead, they've started watering my flowers. My poor hydrangeas. I spent so long growing them, and these kids have already drowned three pots this year!
Gah...
You've had so many visitors lately. I'd wager the Minister came and showed you the draft guest list of the Feast, didn't he?
It's rare to see the True Lung even once a year. I'm sure many would treasure the chance to learn the latest court gossip.
I have no reason to meet the young lad. He's the Minister, after all. What does he need my input for?
Come now, Grandfather. You served for decades in the Ministry of Rites. Your achievements were many, and your legacy is secure.
That's all in the past. If we keep focusing on the past, how will we deal with the present?
Mr. Ning, you have a guest.
I told you, no visitors. Who is it, anyways?
He says he is the Advisor of Yumen.
Hmm...?
The Advisor of Yumen? Never heard of him...
I don't care who he is. I said no visitors, and I meant it.
Grandfather, wait a moment. Might the advisor be here to see me?
Good sir, our master is not accepting visitors right now. Please come back another time.
Please tell Ning, err, your master, that an old friend is here.
Sir, no offense... but judging by your age, are you sure you're an 'old friend' of the master's?
It's true. I dare not make up stories.
Sir, please, be on your way. The master is not accepting any guests right now. If you really are an 'old friend', then I'm sure you wouldn't want to cause trouble, would you?
Mr. Liang...?
Do you not recognize me?
Miss Ning! It's been a while...
I heard you just returned to Baizao. I wanted to see you.
Liang Xun's eyes shift from Miss Ning to the figure standing behind her, the kindly old man.
Don't just stand there, Ciqiu. Invite your friend in for a bit.
Well...
What's the matter? Why isn't anyone eating?
Haha. I feel like the third wheel here, and an old one at that. Maybe I'll just step away for a moment, and the two of you can enjoy your meal?
No, no. You've got it all wrong. It's us young kids who are intruding on your magisterial greatness.
Grandfather, don't scare him like that.
Hahaha. I'm only kidding, Liang Xun, I assure you.
When Ciqiu was still in Shangshu, she used to write me letters, talking about a magistrate there who was both supremely talented and dedicated to his people. Today, I finally got to meet him.
When did I—
Hahaha. It's my birthday in three days. I wasn't planning to go through the trouble of organizing a grand party or anything.
So today, eating dinner and chatting with you two youths is how I will celebrate my birthday.
Let us wish many more years of health and life to your greatness.
Hear hear, Grandfather. Many happy returns.
Alright, alright. Enough of that. Let's eat.
Liang Xun, I thought you were a magistrate from Shangshu. Why have I now heard you are the Advisor of Yumen?
And why have you traveled from Yumen all the way to Baizao?

Yumen's journey was a long one, and the people once living in Yumen have been resettled in batches in different nomadic cities along the route as planned. The last group settled down in Baizao a few days ago.
Wasn't Yumen patrolling the northwestern corridor? How did you end up all the way over here in Baizao?
Dear Grandfather, have you forgotten? Yumen changed its route at the beginning of this year. The new route it was traveling on took it right to Baizao.
As for why Yumen traveled to Baizao, surely you remember that?
Ah... yes... because of that same old problem again.
All those years of fighting, and still no resolution. It's been so long since I retired, and sometimes I still hear those voices in my nightmares. It gives me a headache.
It's a problem that's existed for several millennia now.
Sometimes, issues resolve with the passage of time. Other times, they get worse.
Now I think we need to find a way to solve it. Grandfather, do you have any ideas?
Me? Of course not. If I were to try and intervene, people would accuse me of being a dictator.
Not necessarily. The Minister has been coming to see you ever few days or so. He never mentioned this to you?
Miss Ning, there are some things which your grandfather must keep private, you know.
Hahahaha. My granddaughter sure is direct. That's for sure.

No matter. I am an ordinary citizen now. I am free to speak as I please without fear of divulging secrets, because I do not know any secrets. As for this matter, however, I must defer to the advice of the young.
Well, Liang Xun, what's your solution?
I dare not propose any grand solution. However, there is one thing I do know.
The best strategy is to disrupt your enemy's strategy.
From the court's perspective, war has no upsides, only downsides. From the peoples' perspective, war means death and destruction.
'The best strategy is to disrupt your enemy's strategy.' That makes sense.
But if we always followed this reasoning, the True Lung would not have started 'The Great Hunt' one thousand years ago, and Yan would never have experienced a millennium of prosperity.
Times change, and circumstances change. It's hard to compare things across different historical eras.

That war a thousand years ago was called 'The Great Hunt', but it wasn't really a hunt. It was the Yanese people wresting their future from the hands of the 'gods'. Retreating, even just a little bit, would have resulted in their extinction.
However, things are different now. We are operating from a place of strength. We can and must avoid war at all costs. We cannot allow our soldiers and civilians to suffer any further.
Hahaha. Very well said, young man! You are far more skilled than I ever was, that's for sure.
Goodness no! Forgive me for being so bold, your magisterial greatness.
You say war should be avoided, and I agree. Well then, surely you have an alternate strategy, right? A way to get the situation under control?
I—
Mr. Ning, another visitor has arrived.
What's this now?
She says she is a deputy minister from the Judicial Review Office with urgent business to discuss with you. She insists on coming in.
The Judicial Review Office?
I suppose I will have to let her in.
My sincerest apologies, but I must excuse myself for a moment.
Ciqiu, please continue chatting with our guest here.
A deputy minister? You don't think it's... Lin Qingyan, do you?
You know her too?
Anyone who deals with the Tianshi Bureaus has at least heard of her.
I've heard Bai Dingshan, the Bluethunder Count, is senior to her Shifu. She's skilled in Lei Fa and solves cases as quick as lightning.
But I am not sure why she has come to see my grandfather...
You waited until I was asleep and then locked me in this room! Why?!
I'm not a three-year-old, you know!
Let me out of here!
Dammit! I'm supposed to meet that lady now. I didn't even get her contact, so I can't call her. What am I gonna do?
No. I can't break the door down. They might kick me out of here, and then I'd have no place to stay.
But this window...
I'm on the eighteenth floor, huh? That's not THAT high. Alright. I'll jump!
Miss, you've been standing here for ages. Are you going to make a purchase, or not?
Sir, let me ask you again—this painting here, 'Springtime in the Garden', is it truly an original work by Shujiang?
Miss, you can ask anyone around here. They all know my shop only sells authentic goods. I certainly know how to spot a fake painting.
Look at the technique, the essence of the painting. Who else but Shujiang could have done that?
I'm no art expert, but I see a serious anachronism in this painting.
These grapes on the table here, this particular variety of grapes was not introduced from Gaul into Yan until the year 628. However, this painting is from the year 580. How do you explain that?
Oh come on. That's quite a nit to pick, isn't it? You're just trying to bargain down the price, aren't you?
I've seen your type so many times before. I'm warning you. You won't like me when I'm angry.
Good sir, I never intended to buy your painting in the first place. Why would I be trying to bargain with you?
Besides, what I said about the grapes is true. Don't take my word for it. Look it up, if you don't believe me.
If you think I'm wrong, then prove it. Get as angry as you want; it won't turn your fake painting into a real one.
If you don't want to buy it, then why are you standing here?!
I swear to you, if I'm selling any fakes in this shop, may Heaven strike me down where I stand!
Why is there a canopy above the upper floor?
Blaze? How did you—
Ha... haha... I...
I'm not late, am I?
Sorry for making you wait. I've had so many guests visiting lately. Apologies.
You are...?
My name is Lin Qingyan. I am a deputy minister with the Judicial Review Office. I came here to ask you a few questions, Mr. Ning.
The Judicial Review Office, you say? I haven't talked to anyone there in many, many years. I'm not even sure who's in charge there anymore. Is it Yu Cheng?
Yu Cheng...? No. I'm afraid you're mistaken. Deputy Minister Yu is no longer working with us.
Ah, that's right. Now I remember. One of the Bluethunder Count's Lei Fa students transferred from the Tianshi Bureaus to the Judicial Review Office. Well, that must be you, yes?
I heard that Tianshi Bai was transferred back to Baizao a while ago, yet he still hasn't come to see me, not even for some tea or a bite to eat. I guess he doesn't have time for this old retiree.
These are extraordinary times we are living in. The Tianshi Bureaus must be extremely busy. I'll mention it the Count the next time I see him.
Haha. Sorry for going off on a tangent there. Please, sit down and have some tea.
They sit down in an elegant room that's tastefully decorated and furnished. There seems to be an empty space on the wall where a painting should be hanging.
The part of the wall that had once been covered by the painting is a slightly different color, which makes it stick out even more.
Mr. Ning, the tea is elegant, and so are you. Everything in this room is excellent, but something's missing... a painting, perhaps?
Yes. The missing painting is, in fact, a 'longevity painting' that someone gave me many years ago.
It's supposed to help you live longer. But as the saying goes, the older you grow, the heavier birthdays feel. I believe it's served its purpose.
I would not have expected you to be superstitious, Mr. Ning.
Growing old has left me at fate's mercy, hardly free to act as I wish. It must make you laugh, yes?
But I heard that a thief tampered with one of your paintings recently.

They 'sealed the lips' of the painting with honey. It's an old folk custom to celebrate the New Year. They do this in hopes that the Stove God 'will only say good things and not bad things'. Whoever the thief was, they had some other motive in mind besides theft.
Why did you not report this vandalism?
If I never reported it, then how do you know about it?
One of my colleagues is handling this case. I saw the case file. But the one who first reported it... I don't believe was anyone from your estate...
Well, I'm glad there's at least someone out there who cares about an old man like me.
I would not have expected you to believe in those folk tales, Deputy Minister Lin.
Haha. I am a mere citizen. You, on the other hand, are a mandarin, and your time is valuable. You came here today to ask me something. Just go ahead and ask me directly, please.
I came here to ask you about someone, not something.
Do you remember an individual named Gu Quan?
Grkh...
Ten years ago, Gu Quan accompanied some Yanese dignitaries on an official visit to Victoria. They encountered a Catastrophe, a blizzard, along the way, and poor Gu drowned in a river.
Have you any memory of that, Mr. Ning?
Yes. How could I forget?
I believe it was December. It definitely happened in the winter. By the time the news reached Baizao, it was almost the new year.

I remember the Messenger told us that, after the storm came in, the envoys got trapped on the mountain. Gu Quan stayed at the back of the line, helping shepherd everyone else across the river. In the end, he's the one who ended up falling in.
Sigh. The river must have been freezing cold!
It looks like that incident made quite an impact on you.
Gu Quan was a very good worker. He served the Ministry very well. He was very well-read and had excellent calligraphy.
It's a pity he had no family. The only remnant of him is a cenotaph in Cemetery of the Virtuous. Every year, the Ministry of Rites sends someone to tend it and pay their respects...
No family?
Look at me, blathering on again. Well, anyway, why do you mention Gu Quan?

A Yanese diplomat dying in a foreign country... that is a major international incident. According to the Laws of Yan, both Yan and the other country must send experts to conduct a joint investigation and compile a final report.
However, after Gu Quan's death, the Ministry of Rites did no such thing. Even more startling is that the Judicial Review Office's records of the case are remarkably sparse.
I'm confused. This is clearly a question for the Judicial Review Office. Why are you asking me? I was in the Ministry of Rites.
Well then, let me remind you.
Two months before the team left on their mission, Gu Quan was just an ordinary ninth-rank mandarin. How did he suddenly get promoted to envoy, much less be sent on a mission to Victoria?
Well, you are the one who signed Gu Quan's transfer order. At the time, you were the Minister of Rites.
Hahaha. Now I understand.
You're saying that I deliberately arranged for Gu Quan to go on a diplomatic mission to Victoria, and then 'disposed' of him.
No. That's not what I'm saying.
There is something strange going on here. I intend to solve these cases one-by-one, and I humbly ask for your guidance, Mr. Ning.
Since you're so curious about all this, why not ask me a few more pointed questions?

Do you think it's odd that I, as a former Minister of Rites, would trouble myself over a mere ninth-rank ceremonial officer? Are you implying, perhaps, that he discovered some unspeakable secret about me?
So you're just here to ask me about a ten year old case?
Hahaha... no matter, no matter. Ask whatever you wish.
Just remember, if you keep asking the same questions, you will keep getting the same answers.
What kind of answers do you want?
Truthful ones.
Hahaha. I already have one foot in the grave. What reason do I have to deceive you?
Then again, if you suspect me of committing so many crimes, then you surely don't view my testimony as credible...
I just want you to tell me why Gu Quan was suddenly transferred. Once you tell me that, we'll be able to figure out the rest of it.
Okay, okay. You came all this way to see me, so I think I should tell you what you want to know.
Gu Quan was indeed transferred for a reason: because he specifically requested it.
He wanted the transfer?

Yes, he said that he was ill and was afraid he didn't have much time left, so he wanted to return to his hometown in Victoria to live out the rest of his days. So, I agreed to let him join the delegation to Victoria.
What illness did he have?
I'm not clear on the details either. I only vaguely remember that Gu Quan did seem to be in poor spirits around that time.
But, as such a low-level mandarin, Gu Quan would not have had many opportunities to even meet you. How did he make such a request?
To be honest, it's a bit embarrassing... but there was a hint of selfishness on my part as well.
Gu Quan had magnificent penmanship, so I sometimes had him write letters for me. I also asked him to serve as my children's calligraphy tutor.
He had done so much for me, I felt I owed him a favor. Besides, I did not feel his request stemmed from greed.
That's all there is to say about Gu Quan's transfer. Do you have any other questions for me?
Since I've heard it directly from you, I'll take what you're saying at face value.
However, I disagree with one thing you said.
What?
Anyone who wants to know the truth, be they a mandarin or an ordinary citizen, does not need a special reason for doing so.
Please know, Mr. Ning, that I am indeed only after the truth. I have no other motives.
Now I shall take my leave, sir.
Good day, madam.
Say. Where is Liang Xun?
It's rather late, Grandfather. I saw the young man out many hours ago.
My word. You invited him in to have dinner, and then you had him wait there forever. Tis a poor way to treat a guest.
Yes. Where are my manners? Tell him I apologize, and please let him know that he's welcome to come back any time. Next time, I will be a proper host. I owe him that much.
Liang is a reasonable man. I am sure he'll understand.
By the way, his name is Liang Xun, right? Which 'Xun' is that? How do you write it?
It's that Xun with three dots on the left.
Uh, that one in 'Xun mei qie yi'... A spray of spring, gifted to you. Such a beautiful name; I'm rather fond of it.
I've heard that people with the three dots in their name characters are as clever and swift as water.
Funny. The others all say he's as dull as a board.
The old man walks to the table, picks up a pen, and adds another name to the invite list.
Hmm... maybe he is rather dull...
He came all this way to see his 'sweetheart', but look at the gifts he brought: Yumen's Tea, some strange liquor called 'Liedaozi'. These seem more like gifts meant for me.
Lin Qingyan, wait.
Who's there?
Lin Qingyan gets a strange feeling she's rarely felt before.
A woman with a sword advances to within a zhang of Lin Qingyan, yet she doesn't even notice her...
Until she is standing right in front of her.
Your uniform...
You... you're... an Imperial Guard?
Blaze, what do you think of this one?
Mm... not bad!
What I mean is, do these tangerine cakes taste like the ones you remember?
Umm... well... not exactly.
They're still not as sweet as the ones I had before. They also don't have quite the same mouth feel.
That's strange. This place uses traditional cooking methods. Their snacks are definitely authentic. They haven't changed their recipes in one hundred years.
One hundred years? Have tangerine cakes even been around that long?
No, tangerine cakes are not quite that old, but many famous Baizao snacks do have quite a long history.
The tangerine cake you are looking for were actually invented by a scholar who came to Baizao to study in its early years.
The scholar's hometown was famous for its tangerines. After he moved to Baizao, he missed those tangerines. It was too far to go home and get them, so he improvised.
He smashed up some old, dried tangerine peels and made them into dough, added some sugar, shortening, and spices, and baked them. It was a cheap, easy way to get his tangerine fix.
He gave the recipe to his landlord in lieu of paying rent. He even wrote a small poem about them, 'These tangerine cakes are bright and fresh, just like the orange groves back home.'
After the scholar passed the imperial examination and assumed office, the tangerine cakes became very famous. People began to view them as a symbol of homesickness.
Interesting...
Symbolic food. First time I've ever heard something like that.

Yan is a vast land, with many different climate zones, each with their own cash crops and produce. That's why there are many different types of Yanese cuisine. You can trace all of Yan's history through its food.
For example, Yan's Four Major Cuisines match up with its Four Great Restaurants, which predate Baizao's transformation into a nomadic city.
Cities develop, but flavors remain. It would be odd to find any snack without its own bit of history behind it.
One of my colleagues told me that there are many different kinds of Tianshis in Yan. You know so much about food, you must be some kind of Tasty Food Tianshi, right?
If there really was such an individual, someone who could use Arts to transform stones into vegetables, that might be worth studying, eh?
Alas, I am just an amateur foodie. I like to take notes on the food I eat, and its history. Over the years, I've compiled several notebooks full of such information.
To put it another way, Blaze, if there really were a better recipe for tangerine cakes out there, surely someone here would know about it.
But I'm telling you! The ones I had when I was younger, they were sweeter, and just better!
That really is rather odd...
Traditional tangerine cakes emphasize the fresh essence of the tangerines, not the taste of their pulp. Very little sugar is used, because tangerine cakes are supposed to be a light snack.
You're telling me you've eaten much sweeter tangerine cakes, and I'm telling you that I've never heard of such a thing.
Maybe we can ask one of the chefs around here to try cooking up a batch with extra sugar, so you can compare?
But even if we do that, it doesn't prove that the sweeter variety of tangerine cakes ever actually existed before.
What I want to know is, how did the taste of these tangerine cakes change during their journey from Baizao to Victoria. I'm sure there's a story there somewhere.
It seems you're just as much of a foodie as I am.
I was afraid you'd get tired of traipsing off to all these restaurants.
You're the guide. You lead, I follow.
Alright then. Let's keep looking!
My word, look at the time! We should have lunch first.
Hmm? Lunch?
Yes. It's lunchtime. Shouldn't we have some lunch?
But I just finished eating snacks from five different restaurants!
Snacks are snacks, but lunch is lunch. You must have a proper lunch, you know!
I don't think my tummy has any more room for lunch...
Haha. I know one restaurant that I can guarantee won't make your tummy ache.
Will you give it a try?
You're the foodie here, so I, and my stomach, trust you!
Hmm?
What's wrong?
I feel like... we're being followed.
You... you're... an Imperial Guard?
No, no, no. Without darksteel armor, I'm not a proper member of the Imperial Guards. Officially, I'm a captain serving with the Yulin Guards.
Deputy Minister Lin, I doubt you'd want to encounter anyone wearing darksteel armor anyway, certainly not in broad daylight.
I once heard Taihe mention that there is a 'junior drillmaster' serving in the ranks the Imperial Guard apprentices. She's very young, but skilled beyond her years.
That must be you. You're the junior drillmaster.
Hmph. I guess Taihe's still sore that I beat him in a couple matches last month. There's no need for name calling, though.
No matter. I'd like to ask you, what are you doing here?
I'm investigating a case.
Does that case have anything to do with the former Minister of Rites? Has the Judicial Review Office issued you a search warrant?
You're just a drillmaster. You have no authority to question whatever warrants I may or may not have, do you?
You have no authority to question my authority. Just answer my questions, please.
...Very well.
I heard you were on a long business trip just recently. You left Baizao for a while, did you not?
That's right. I went to go question a witness in an old case and update the case files.
What old case? From when? What witness? Why are you just investigating it now?
I don't understand your line of questioning.
If the Yulin Guards want to investigate the Judicial Review Office, I'll go submit the necessary documentation immediately.
