If that still isn't enough for you, I can have the Minister of the Judicial Review Office report to the Yulin Guards for questioning.
My questions have nothing to do with the Judicial Review Office. They only concern you, Deputy Minister Lin.
So you went to investigate an old case. What did you learn?
...Too much time has passed, so the trail has gone cold. I didn't learn anything.
Nothing? Really? Are you sure?
Nothing.
Alright. I believe you. However, remember that you are speaking to the Yulin Guards here. Your answers carry great weight.
Very good. I'm done discussing official business for now.
So you still have some personal business you wish to discuss?
Taihe, that dumb Forte, told me he doesn't have many friends in government. Well, you now count as one.
Well, I must warn you, don't stick your neck out too far.
Your worries are unfounded. I'm just pursuing the truth here, as it is my job to do. I'm not putting myself at any additional risk.
...Very good.
Give Taihe my regards.
They are questioning why I'm here. Well, why are the Yulin Guards here?
Their presence here, I think, is proof that I'm on the right track.
Phew—
Boy, am I stuffed. I never would have guessed that a roadside stand could make such amazing wontons. They're certainly better than the ones in the Rhodes Island cafeteria freezer!
I'm so stuffed that I want to just take a nap right here. Ah... nng...
After eating all that greasy street food, a nice, hearty bowl of wontons is the perfect thing to settle your stomach.
The famous restaurants need no introduction. As your guide in Baizao, I figured I'd share some of my favorite lesser-known food spots.
Well I'm lucky to have found such a good tour guide. Thank you!
Well, I'm also glad to have found a culinary companion. I tend to eat alone, which means I can only order one or two entrees for myself.
You know what they say, 'the more the merrier.' Now that I'm here, you can order as much food as you want.
Speaking of food, what is in these wontons, anyway? They're fantastic!
Just meat and celery. The key ingredient is the soup!
Most places use shellbeast caviar in wonton soup to enhance the flavor. Unfortunately, the finniness of the caviar crowds out the other flavors.
This place uses soybean sprouts instead. This makes for a milder soup, which allows the flavor of the wontons to shine!
The meat filling for the wontons is fresh beast belly. After the wontons are cooked, the bits of fat will naturally seep into the soup.
These wontons are light, yet delicious. They are made of high-quality ingredients which are hard to find elsewhere.
Well, do these wontons have a history? Do tell.
Not really. I just really like this particular wonton shop, I guess.
When I get writer's block, or need a little pick-me-up, I come here and eat a bowl of wontons.
The shopkeeper has been making wontons her whole life. She used to own this shop together with her husband.
Her husband fell ill and passed away, leaving her to raise her child alone. Later, her son joined the army, but he died in a Catastrophe up north several years ago.
She's still running the place all by herself. She says she wants to continue the family legacy and preserve their authentic taste.
What a sad story.
There are many such sad stories. There is no joy without pain.

Over the years, as I've been coming to this shop, I've helped the lady with an errand or two. In return, she told me her family's secret recipe. She hopes that one day, after she's gone, I can pass on her craft to the next generation.
I have something I want to ask you.
Are you really going through all this trouble with the tangerine cakes just because you made a bet with the Young Chef?
Why do you ask?
I simply noticed that you weren't terribly focused on the taste of the delicacies at all. It seemed like you had some other purpose in mind.
Was it that obvious? Wow...
When you watch people eat, you see into their souls.
Sorry, I was just curious. If I've intruded on your privacy, I sincerely apologize.
Hey now! We're friends! I'm not keeping secrets from you.
You're right. I'm not really that concerned about the bet.
Those tangerine cakes, well, my father used to send them to me when he was living in Baizao.
Your father, was he Yanese?
Err... meh, whatever. I don't have anything to hide from you.
I grew up in Victoria, and my father is Yanese. One year, he suddenly returned to Yan for some reason.
I never saw him again, and he died over a decade later in a land foreign to me.
I'm sorry, Blaze...
It's not your fault. Why do you keep apologizing?
I didn't think much about it at first, but yesterday when you mentioned that sweet tangerine cakes are unusual, I thought, 'someone who knows how to make those cakes might have also known my dad.'
Your dad… was also…
He was also what?
Oh... nothing...
Well, I promise you that I will do everything in my power to help you find out the truth about your father—
You're such a contradiction—so bold when you eat, yet so refined when you speak.
When we're eating together, it's all good fun; we're good friends. Why do you turn into a different person as soon as you put down your chopsticks?
Friends?
And another thing, why are you helping me look for these tangerine cakes in the first place?
Well, I'm writing a book.
Most cookbooks today only contain recipes from renowned chefs, yet the streets and the countryside are full of unique delicacies that few people ever pay heed.
I hope to collect all these recipes into a book. Perhaps, years later, people will read this book and learn of Baizao's unique cuisine. Hm... it's just a silly dream of mine, is all.
And also...
What?
I think I'll keep that a secret for now.
Well, as long as we're out here, you might as well take me to a few more places, yeah?
Sure! Where do you want to go?
Let me see... this letter mentions the Virtue Bookstore, the Humble Garden, and the Baizao Academy.
Hey... what does this word mean?
Can you let me have a look?
This is a letter? Why is it so greasy?
I didn't do that! It was like that when I opened it! I don't know what my dad was doing when he wrote it.
So these are all letters that your father wrote to you?
Yes. The few that I managed to save.
(Strange, this stationery... it's...)
Hmm? What is it?
N-Nothing. It's just that most of the places mentioned in this letter no longer exist.
Huh? But it was only written a decade ago.
For Baizao, a decade is enough for the whole city to change.
Have you noticed that anything has changed since arriving in Baizao?
Well, aside from the food, there are all these energy poles all over the place. It seems like kind of a waste.
It may seem wasteful, but in fact, we have no other choice.
The city of Baizao was founded a long, long time ago.
At that time, Yan had just experienced a great war, and the country was in dire straits. However, suddenly, an extremely rich Originium vein was discovered right next to the ruins of a battlefield.
Wow. What impeccable timing! You Yanese sure are lucky!
No. It wasn't lucky at all. The Originium vein was rich... too rich. It attracted too many Originium particles in one place, which eventually led to Catastrophe.
Basically, not only was it difficult to mine, but it also created a vast stretch of land that was prone to Catastrophes, and there was nothing the Yanese could do about it.

That is, until one of the Tianshis had an idea—they could build a city right on top of the Originium vein. The city infrastructure would deplete the excess Originium energy, preventing further Catastrophes.
Building a city on top of an Originium mine? That sounds like a recipe for disaster!
It was indeed an extremely dangerous undertaking. However, the True Lung decided to go ahead with it... and that's how Baizao was founded.
How did they do it? I can't even imagine...
Honestly, I'm not totally sure either. In all the Yanese history books, there is only a single passage that talks about that period—

'Skilled individuals were sent to the disaster zone, and after fifty-three years of effort, a city was built and established as the capital. The city was named Baizao, with the hope of tending all cooking stoves under heaven.'
As for the more advanced technical details, you'd probably have to look at some of the materials from the Tianshi Bureaus. That's beyond my area of expertise.
So to this day, the Baizao government still encourages us to use this energy.
Baizao is constantly changing. That's how these skyscrapers appeared, as well as the numerous nomadic city plates.
And what happened to the people who built the city?
*sigh*...
I'm completely stuffed, but I still haven't found the taste I'm looking for.

I've taken you to every restaurant I know that sells such cakes.
Maybe tomorrow we can go look in some of the larger restaurants.
*burp*
What did I tell you? What did I tell you?
You looked the whole day, yet you couldn't find your so-called, 'authentic tangerine cakes', could you?
You were following us all day?
You were afraid we would find better food, weren't you?
Me? Afraid? Pah!
I already told you before, if you're looking for the best chef in Baizao, you've already found him. If I can't cook it, no one else can.
Blaze, I've tasted his food. It really is quite excellent. A rare treat.
You really know how to flatter!
Besides, you're arguing over the details in how a single pastry is made—how could that possibly be the standard for judging a chef's skill?
Still, I can see that you, chef, are still curious about these sweet tangerine cakes that Blaze is talking about, are you not?
....Not really.
How about this? You two set aside your bet, and we'll see where we can find these sweet tangerine cakes.
That way, Blaze will find the tangerine cakes she's looking for, and you, chef, will learn a new recipe. Sounds like a delightful arrangement, no?
Huh? What?
What about my one month of free meals?
Hmph, my cooking skills are so superb that no one can match them, but there are other chefs that I respect. If I had to name one in particular, it would be...
Well... it would be...
Who?
The head chef of Ding Feng Lou, the highest rated of the Four Great Restaurants.
You mean, Mr. Mo Bufu?
Everyone at Ding Feng Lou is busy preparing for the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies. That means the old man is prepping for his last feast.
His last feast? You mean, he's retiring?
Who knows! That old man's been challenging people left and right lately—really showing off...
What are you waiting for? Let's go!
It's not so simple...
Chef Mo is quite an important man. He is responsible for the entire feast. We can't bother him with trifling matters such as some tangerine cakes.
The girl is clever, but she's only half right. If you really do want to go meet him, I have a way.
Last month's receipts?
No. Not in this pocket...
Ah. Here it is.
An invitation to the Ding Feng Lou Chef Selection...
Blaze, we won't get many opportunities to meet Chef Mo.
Then what are we waiting for? Whoever is going to the competition, take me with you!
As to who will participate in the competition...
Huh? What are you looking at me for?

lines.st: Oh, you came to the library too. What a coincidence.
Hello, Yu. Are you here to look up some case files?
Just grabbing some books to put under my pillow. I haven't been sleeping well lately—maybe some reading will help.
How I envy the life of a student. Their brains are filled with books, not worries.
Surely you're joking.
I'm not joking at all. Investigating these sordid cases for the Judicial Review Office every day is bound to make one feel sick.
They say the Baizao Academy library is the cleanest and purest place in the world, where only wise men and sages dwell. So I'll sit here for a while and purify my soul.
Did you use to study here at the Academy too, Gu Quan?
That was many years ago...
I still haven't achieved anything yet, academically, since I graduated from here. I feel unworthy of the academy's prestige.
But I was fortunate to meet a great teacher here, and that experience will benefit me for the rest of my life.
Who is this teacher you speak of?
...No longer with us.
Then why did you come here today? To borrow books?
I'm here to look up some case files...
The Ministry of Rites is preparing for a grand celebration, and we need to refer to some old rituals.
Hah...
I'm a deputy minister for the Judicial Review Office. Do you take me for blind, or merely illiterate?
That thing you have in your hand is clearly the case file from fifteen years ago, when the Grand Preceptor committed treason!
You know that file is top secret, right? You really have some gall!
I... have nothing to hide.
I am indeed looking for materials related to that old case. I think there may be more to it than we realize. If you think I'm doing anything illegal, then by all means, have me arrested.
What are you blabbering about? Did I say I was going to arrest you? I was only saying...
that it's quite a coincidence, because I'm also very interested in that case.

lines.x: I learned a lot from our debate today. However, I still maintain my own views about the new reforms...
I'll have to do more research on this.

It's fine. This campus is plenty large enough to accommodate a wealth of different ideas. If my arguments have not convinced you, then you should not force yourself to agree with me just because I'm a teacher.
The students of this academy must focus on real world issues, instead of getting lost in theoretical or ideological musings.
Duoxie.
A clock hangs on the top of the ancient building, the minute hand ticking as the shadow of sunlight moves slowly. The bell rings, and soon, students start pouring out of their classrooms.
A lone figure moves against the tide. The guards fail to notice the unfamiliar face amidst the crowd.
Good day. Do you need some help?
Hello, how do I get to the archives where the students' files are stored?
You mean the registrar? What do you want with those files?
I'm a new assistant lecturer. The professor wants me to organize the graduates' files.
That's odd...
Which professor?
Professor Jing. She teaches military theory at the college affiliated to the Ministry of War.
She's teaching classes all day today, so she asked me to do this errand for her.
Oh!
What's wrong?
Go straight down here, pass the main building, turn right, and you'll see the Bingshen building. That's it.
You'd better get going... Trust me, you don't want to keep her waiting.
...Duoxie.
Ahem, the first rule of any restaurant is that once you enter the kitchen, the chef's words are law.
If you want to win the contest and meet Mo Bufu, you must follow my instructions to the letter. Do you understand?
Didn't we agree to split the prize money seventy-thirty? Why are you acting like I owe you a favor?
Okay Okay. Enough of that. Didn't you say you wanted to learn a few new recipes anyway?
Have you changed your mind?
No. I want to do this, but I'm no Rhodes Island five-star chef. This competition sounds too difficult for an amateur like me.
Why don't you go instead?
I am the head chef of Savors at Yu's. Participating in this kind of competition would be sullying my good name.
I'm giving you an opportunity here. As my apprentice, you can go show your skills, and wipe that arrogant smirk off Mo Bufu's face.
Wouldn't Xingzhu be a better choice? She's a foodie, you know.
I'm afraid that, as The Wandering Foodie, I'm too well known. It would be inappropriate for me to participate in a food competition.
Enough chitchat. First thing's first. To get you ready for this competition, let me tell you a little about Ding Feng Lou.
I've been hearing about this place since I first set foot in Baizao. What's so special about it?
Remember I told you about the Four Great Restaurants of Baizao, corresponding to the four main branches of Yanese Cuisine, that is, North, South, East, and West?
About four hundred years ago, several chefs arrived in Baizao. They each set up their own restaurants and culinary sects. This marked the founding of the four main branches of Yanese cuisine.
It was the first head chef of Ding Feng Lou who promoted the founding in the first place.
The legendary chef's name has been lost to history. He was the founder of Northern Cuisine, but was also proficient in the other three styles.
The other three chefs were only invited to Baizao after they lost to the Northern Chef in a cooking competition.
With such a storied history, Ding Feng Lou outshined the other three restaurants and became known as the 'number one restaurant in Baizao.'
Who's teaching this class, you or me?
Oh. My apologies!

Hmph, of course, what she said is pretty much accurate. The only thing I'll add is that eventually, the Four Great Restaurants of Baizao started fighting with each other for fame and glory. These squabbles continue to this day, and they're really quite annoying.
I thought Baizao was a place where all these great chefs could learn from each other. If I had known there was going to be all this infighting, I would not have gone...
Not have gone where?

Ahem...!
In any case, what you need to know is that, as the most famous restaurant in Baizao, Ding Feng Lou, and its Chef Selection Competition, has extremely high standards.

Ding Feng Lou has not taken on any new disciples in many years. The only reason they're hosting this competition now is because they suddenly got short-handed right when they needed to start preparing for the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies.
But I've also heard that Chef Mo is advancing in age and still hasn't found an apprentice to take over the business...
The format of the competition is different each time, but there is one requirement that stays constant: each chef has to showcase their best dish.
Wait a second...
If the competition is really that difficult, how can I ever hope to win?
Logic would dictate that an amateur chef with absolutely no culinary experience would have little hope of winning.
However, you have me in your corner. I can teach you a recipe that is easy to cook and is perfect for the occasion—
Longevity noodles.
Longevity noodles? Why would they serve longevity noodles at the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies?
The reigning True Lung's birthday just happens to coincide with the Mid-Autumn Festival, so every year, they serve longevity noodles at the Feast.
But it's such a simple dish. Is it really appropriate for a major competition like this?
Who told you that simple dishes can't show great skills?
Just watch and learn.
What's that smell...? That smells wonderful! What is it?
Hm-hmm—
What do you think?
It's like magic! You really only used the ingredients you showed us before? Nothing else?
Fresh shellbeast meat, mushrooms, scallions, bok choy...
It's a simple recipe with basic ingredients, but the result is truly amazing.
Sometimes, the true skill of a chef hides in unseen places. There's more to this bowl of noodles than meets the eye.

Another chef can use these exact same ingredients, but if the ratios, the heat, or the cooking time vary just a little bit, it'll taste completely different. Do you know how long I spent perfecting this recipe?
Don't just stand there. Dig in!
Wow.
I take back any criticism I had of your food!
You just inhaled it! Did you even taste the flavor?!
If Blaze can actually replicate that taste, maybe she really can meet Chef Mo.
But are you really up to the task, Blaze?
Figuring out the secret to this recipe would be difficult indeed. But simply following its steps should not be too much trouble.
Have you ever cooked anything before?
Of course!
Ah. That's good then.
Now I'll give you the recipe and explain every step for you. Follow them exactly, and you'll be fine.
Great! I'll study hard! I won't disappoint you!
Didn't you say you knew how to cook?!
The instant noodles are already pre-made! How should I know how to knead flour into dough?
You really are a hopeless case!
You haven't really even taught me anything yet...
How can I teach you anything? You managed to break three bowls just while kneading the dough! I can't risk you breaking anything else here!
Aiyo... aiyo...
Be careful... These bowls are expensive... We can't afford to buy new ones—
*sigh*

lines.x: Why are there no records at all?

lines.st: Search\tGu Quan

No results
Search\tGu Quan

No results

lines.x: What is going on?
Gu Quan, an ethnic Yanese born and raised in Victoria, returned to Yan in 1074. In 1076, he was admitted to Baizao Academy. After completing his studies, he joined the Ministry of Rites...
These are the only records left for the deceased in that case.
They should have files for all mandarins who graduated from the Imperial Academy and went to work in the Six Ministries of the court… but why can't I find him in the system?
Gu Quan, what did you...?
Professor, I was just trying to confirm that with you. I didn't mean to ask you to come here in person...
She said she's a new assistant professor? Why haven't I ever heard of her before?
The person who came here seemed to know you very well, so I didn't...
How many times have I told you? The Academy's personnel files are confidential! Keep that in mind!
Forget it. I'll go handle this myself.
Oh no!
Hmm?
The room is empty and the documents on the desk are still where they were, without any signs of having been moved.
Qingyan, you're a bit too old to be playing hide-and-seek, aren't you?
J-Jing...
Has the Judicial Review Office come to our school to apprehend some culprit?
The Office is investigating a case, so I've come here to collect a few old case files. Please, Jing... Professor Jing... Please cooperate.
Alright then. If you're here on official business, show me your official documentation.
I rushed so quickly to get here that I left it back at my office.
Really? Well then I'll just place a call in to the head of the Office.
Wait... wait a moment!
Professor, are you okay?
Should I call security?
*sigh*
(gives a pleading look)
No. I forgot that I asked some other professor here to collect some materials for me.
I'm sorry for wasting your time.
It's not a problem! Goodbye, professor!
Alright. Now it's just the two of us.
Xie... Xiexie.
*sigh* Qingyan...
I have always thought that you were a well-mannered and trustworthy girl. Every time I mentioned you to Le, I told him to follow your example.
And look at you now—What are you doing here? A deputy minister from the Judicial Review Office, sneaking around like this?
I'm conducting an investigation...
What kind of case requires this secrecy? What kind of case can't go through the normal process?
A cold case... the deceased was once a student at the Baizao Academy, but I just searched the personnel files and found no record of him.
The Academy has a record of every student. This person you speak of, how do you know they once studied here?
I saw it in the files at the Judicial Review Office.
The Judicial Review Office collects its files directly from the Ministry of Rites. However, the Ministry's files do not match up with the Academy's. Someone made a mistake somewhere.
Is there a chance that someone altered the Academy's files?
How could that possibly have happened?
The Academy is one of the Yanese government's most important talent pipelines. Once a student's information is entered into the system, it is archived under strict supervision.
No one has the authority to modify this data, unless...
Unless what...?
No. That's impossible.
Enough of this nonsense. You'd better leave now.
It's almost mealtime. Pretty soon, lots of students will be crowding these halls. If anyone else sees you here...
Also, please don't go doing something like this again.
Mealtime...
We need to go back to Baizao to investigate. Why are you looking at all these travel guides?
These places... I've seen them in my dad's letters.
Humble Garden, Virtue Bookstore... oh and when he was studying here, there was a Shangshu Diner near the main entrance. He said they have the best twice-cooked pork in town!
Now that I'm visiting Baizao, I want to visit all the places he mentioned to me.
Say, is there a restaurant near campus called the Shangshu Diner?
Huh? You want Shangshu food?
The place you're talking about closed more than thirty years ago. How would you possibly know about it?
More than thirty years ago? Are you sure?
Yes. It closed in 1065 when I was in school. How could I forget?
Lots of students used to go eat there, because it was near campus and served tasty, affordable food.

But then, in 1065, there was a terrible Catastrophe in Shangshu, and the restaurant owner had to return home to deal with the aftermath. The students even made donations to help with the rescue effort.
A man who was born and raised in Victoria and did not come to Yan until 1074... how could he know about the place?
Unless...
Unless he had visited Yan before, or maybe he wasn't born and raised in Victoria after all? Maybe he was a native Yanese?
What are you mumbling about?
If Gu Quan's Ministry of Rites records were modified, but he was indeed a student at the Academy, well then, there would definitely be something worth digging up in the Academy's files.
What year was Blaze born? If we work back from there...
Does this system have the records of students enrolled between 1052 and 1062?
You...
Help me just this once. It's extremely important...
Thousands of students graduate each year. You want to look through ten years of records at once? Do you know how long that will take?
The person in question worked for the Ministry of Rites. He probably studied at an affiliated college. That may help narrow the search a bit.
If the person you are looking for was not a high-ranking mandarin, you may want to start looking at files after 1055.
That year, the Academy was restructured. It became a true public education institution, instead of just serving as a talent pipeline for the government. The enrollment figures shot up.
I see... Duoxie!
Go. You only have two hours.
Found you...
...Gu Sheng.

Male, born in Langyi in 1037, accepted into Baizao Academy in 1055. He was assigned to the Education Department administered by the Ministry of Rites... and was among the first batch of students to enroll after the school was restructured.
Top marks in all subjects... Wait. He didn't graduate?
This file was last updated in... 1062?
This is a missing persons file?
'Gu Sheng, along with his wife and daughter, disappeared in 1062. Accordingly, this file has been sealed.'
So he was already a family man when he was in Baizao... and all three of them disappeared...
1062... Is there something special about 1062?
Blaze... I need to confirm something...
You were born in 1062, in Victoria, right?
Yes. From as far back as I can remember, I lived in Victoria.
I asked you where you were born!
How am I supposed to remember that?
Gu Quan... Gu Sheng...
Qingyan, your time is almost up. You should get going.
What did he say?
No good. The dough still won't do. Let's try again.
But that's already my twentieth batch!
Don't worry, you have been practicing all day, and you're starting to iron out the issues with your technique. Success is not far away.
I'm really sorry. You don't need to stay here with me through all this.
It's not a problem!
You've been helping me so much, I guess you must also be interested in the outcome of all of this, huh?
Alright. Just wait here a minute. This time I'm sure I'll do it, and I'll whip you up a big, nice bowl of noodles for dinner.
Did you... have something else you wanted to say to me just now?
What?
N-Never mind...
To be fair, the recipe the Young Chef gave you was indeed simple enough. He probably didn't expect that your biggest difficulty would be kneading the dough.
Look at you defending him!
I'm sorry...
I'm doing all I can do. It's hard. When I work, my body temperature rises, and the dough starts to dry out in just a few seconds.
I add more water to the flour, and then more flour to the water, and then more flour and water... The dough that was originally the size of my fist is now almost as big as my head!
The situation with your rising body temperature is certainly unique...
Anyway, you need to think positively: so long as you learn to knead the dough, making the longevity noodles will be no problem at all!
Speaking of which, for someone who has never cooked before, you certainly have some impressive knife skills.
Well, back at Rhodes Island, elite operators like me perform surgery on ourselves in emergency situations.
I guess there's not much difference between using a scalpel and a kitchen knife, assuming you're not using it like the Young Chef does when he chops vegetables.
I can even use the skills I learned from stitching my own wounds to mend my own clothes.
I wouldn't have thought your job would be this dangerous.
Hmph, not a big deal if you have the time to practice.
The more I think about it, the angrier I get. Can't he just have more patience with me?
Hey! Look! I think you're getting the hang of it. The amount of force is just right. Keep it up!
I... haven't even... had any dinner yet!
Hold onto that anger! Fold the dough over and continue kneading. Right. You can get even angrier if you want!
No. I can't get any angrier... boy the noodles he makes sure are delicious!
I'm spent—
Ugh... Why is cooking even more exhausting than combat training?
I understand this is really hard for you...
I don't want to deal with that pipsqueak anymore! Can I be your apprentice instead? You're a gourmand. You must know how to cook, right?
Spare me the compliments... I am nothing close to him when it comes to cooking.
And you know, it's funny that I've never actually eaten longevity noodles before.
They are just noodles, right? You've tried so many different restaurants. How could you have never eaten longevity noodles before?
I've been to many restaurants that serve longevity noodles, but it only makes sense to eat them on your birthday!
I celebrate every birthday at home. They don't allow me to go out on my birthday.
Okay, but even if you celebrate your birthdays at home, your mom or dad never made you a bowl of longevity noodles?
Actually, I've never met my parents...
Oh, I'm sorry...
Although I have never seen my parents, I still have other family members. Every year, on my birthday, my family holds a big banquet for me.
At banquets like that, I don't think they care much about longevity noodles.
However, I've always been a little afraid of birthdays...
Isn't it nice that other people remember your birthday?
I can't really explain it. I always feel that every time I have a birthday party, I will be surrounded by a lot of people who want me to laugh, and also to cry.
But it doesn't seem to matter whether I'm there or not...
Enough talk about me. You're from Victoria. Do they eat longevity noodles there?
I grew up in Victoria, but my parents are both Yanese, at least, by blood.
When I was little, every year, on my birthday, my mom would make me a bowl of longevity noodles. The taste was nothing special. It was just regular old home cooking.
After I left home and could no longer eat my mom's home cooking, I even forgot what it was like to celebrate a birthday.
That is, until that night...
The seasoning packet, the vegetable packet... and I'll add two sausages, hehe... why are the lights off in the cafeteria?
Hey! What are you guys doing here? You scared me!
Shhh, keep your voice down.
It's late. What are you doing in the cafeteria at this hour?
Ace... why do you look all serious? There's not another urgent mission, is there?
What are you holding there? Instant noodles?
I stayed too long at the training ground this afternoon and didn't have enough for dinner!
What? Now that I'm an elite operator, I can't eat instant noodles anymore?
The captain isn't scolding you for eating junk food. He's saying...
Today is your birthday, and you're eating instant noodles?
My birthday...?
But didn't Dr. Kal'tsit say—
That's why I told you to lower your voice.
So long as we don't make too much noise, even if she hears us, she'll pretend not to hear, yes?
I even suspect that she deliberately arranged for our fieldwork breaks to overlap, just so we could throw you a birthday party.
Think of it as her way of giving you a birthday blessing... even if it's hard to imagine.
Blaze, you are one of us, a part of Rhodes Island. You are not alone.
I... you... thank you all so much!
Alright. It's already midnight. Happy birthday, Blaze!
Happy birthday, Big Cat!
Happy Birthday.
Happy birthday! Also, Amiya isn't on the ship right now, but she asked me to pass on the birthday wishes. She hopes you stay happy and healthy!
Aww... Bunny...
So, are you planning to come blow out the candles and cut the cake, or are you going back to your instant noodles?
That was the first time so many people celebrated my birthday with me. That bowl of instant noodles became my longevity noodles.
Everyone knew how much I wanted to have a birthday party, but that day, not a single person actually came out and said, 'I knew you wanted this'.
They acted like... it was just another one of my many, many birthdays.
I liked the 'nothing special' feeling of it.
Now you're making me jealous...
Oh, right! You said you've never had longevity noodles. Well, let me whip up some for you!
But... you haven't even learned how to knead the dough yet...
I don't know how to knead noodles, but I do know how to cook ready-made noodles. I'll go ask Jiang for some dried noodles that I can simply boil.
Wait... handmade noodles... ready-made noodles...
Maybe it really will work, after all!
Early the next morning, the restaurant is still quiet, save for a few fowlbeasts jumping back and forth on the fallen leaves outside the door.
The night watchman at the Judicial Review Office lets out a loud yawn. The fowlbeasts take off in all directions.
*Yawns* Ughh...
I had a nightmare last night that a beckbeast ate all the flour in my kitchen. How terrifying!
Morning!
Have some noodles!
You... figured out how to make them?
Look at the bags under your eyes. Were you practicing all night?
Haha. No. Only about half the night. Hey, Xingzhu still hasn't woken up yet.
Enough talking for now. Try the noodles!
You.... huh?
You gave up learning how to knead the dough and boiled some ready-made noodles instead?!
These are no ordinary ready-made noodles. Xingzhu and I looked through all the ready-made noodles on the market, and picked the ones we feel have a texture closest to your hand-made ones.
Of course, they don't have quite the same mouthfeel as hand-made noodles. So Xingzhu adjusted your soup base recipe to ensure they taste similar to the longevity noodles you make from scratch.
This is blasphemy! You're using ready-made noodles, and you tampered with my recipe? My word!
Don't jump to any conclusions yet. Please, have a taste!
The Young Chef picks up his chopsticks, and, with a giant frown on his face, pulls out some of the noodles. He carefully takes a sniff, then finally takes a taste.
Hmm...
Well...?
Hmm...
Say something!
Why did you two think this would work?
Xingzhu was starving after all my failed attempts! I figured at that point, I was better off just making noodles I knew how to make, so at least she'd have something to eat!
Then I started thinking: the most important element of longevity noodles isn't the taste. Instead, it's making sure the person who eats them feels the birthday blessing.

Some people just aren't skilled enough to make noodles like you do. However, I can still use ready-made noodles to bring birthday blessings to myself or my family. That's the real meaning of longevity noodles, anyway.
See how yellow and shiny these noodles are? They look more lively than yours, right?
Of course, yes, I am taking shortcuts with this dish...
You mentioned birthday blessings. Do you think people can taste that in your noodles?
I... think so?
Last night, after I finally made a proper bowl of noodles for the first time, I shared them with Xingzhu. We felt so happy when eating it. I even caught Xingzhu wiping away tears...
Swapping out the noodles, then adjusting the oil and salt ratios accordingly... Quite some innovation there. Xingzhu sure has a sharp palate.
But the ready-made noodles need to be rinsed with cold water after cooking. You rinsed them, but you didn't drain all the cold water out.
The cold water lowered the temperature of the soup, which also affected the texture of the noodles. If you really want to make good food, you need to be mindful of these little details.
Got it! I'll remember that!
Wait, then you mean...?
Enough. I'm only continuing to teach you because I can see you've put in the effort.
You still need to learn how to make your own noodles from scratch. You don't really expect to use ready-made noodles in the competition, do you?
Hehe. Great!
As your shifu, I'm glad that you've improved. But we still have to see whether the customers recognize your efforts.
Well... what do we do?
Later on, if anyone orders noodles, you'll make them.
If the customer doesn't like the noodles, then you'll need to keep practicing.
I'm on it!
Hello! Would you like to try our special longevity noodles?
It's not my birthday. Why would I want longevity noodles?
It's a new dish we're trying out. We'll give it to you for free!
No. I meant, if you try it, and like it, then maybe you should still pay for it. I mean, I have to settle up with the boss, you know.
That's... an interesting way to do business.
Sure. Give me that bowl of noodles, then.
(eager eyes)
Uhh... do you need something else from me?
No! No! I just want to see how you like the noodles.
Okay okay. I'll give them a try.
The man picks up one single noodle using his chopsticks, then a piece of bok choy, and some scallions. He tries them one after another.
He eats with graceful manners, but somehow, in just a few moments' time, the entire bowl is empty.
What do you think?
Mmmm... Good. Even excellent.
R-Really?
The soup has a rich umami taste, and the ingredients are top notch. If you had let the soup simmer for another day or so, it might have tasted even better.
But I was already so hungry. If I had tried to wait another day for that ultimate perfection, I would have starved...
He wants to create the perfect taste, but the customer cannot wait that long.
Even if I want to wait for him... there are some things that I just can't wait for any longer...
You're saying...
Don't worry about it. Just tell your boss what I said, yeah?
The man stands up. The patterns on his clothes emit a strange glow; the light distorts and blurs his face, so much so that Blaze cannot make out what he looks like.
Hey! You... you didn't pay!
Just deduct the funds from my account.
Regardless of what you're looking for, after you leave here, do not tell anyone you got this file from the Academy. Do you understand?
Yes...
What's wrong? Do you have more questions?
Do you remember... that bloody case... back in 1062?
This isn't the sort of thing you casually bring up in a conversation.
I just happened to think of it.

Is the Grand Preceptor you previously mentioned, the one who supported the academic reforms which benefited countless poor students, the same Grand Preceptor who murdered the emperor and plotted treason forty years ago?
I have no way to answer your question. I will never speak about things that I do not know.

I have never met that Grand Preceptor in person, but I have read some of her writings on political and social issues. Her extensive knowledge, clearly reflected in her works, has been deeply enriching for me.
But people are complicated and cannot be easily categorized.
You're saying you're not completely convinced that the outcome of that case was correct?
Qingyan, you are never good at hiding what you are thinking.
I knew you would...
The Office's archives will not house muddled cases, no matter how many people they involve.
Solving those cases is my duty as deputy minister.
I shall not dissuade you from doing as you see fit. However, you must learn to protect yourself, no matter what happens.

*sigh* Before your parents retreated into seclusion in the mountains, they entrusted me with looking after you. If anything were to happen to you, I would never be able to look them in the eyes again.
I'm not a child. Why do I need people to take care of me?
Everyone feels that way when they are young. However, as a mother, watching Zuo Le grow up, day by day, I feel a weight growing on my heart.
What else should we parents worry about, other than our children's safety?
But if your parents learned that I prevented you from fulfilling your duties out of concern for your 'safety', then they really would blame me.
Duoxie... Sister...
*sigh* You call me your sister, but in my eyes, you have always been like a child of my son's generation.
Speaking of which, Yumen is near Baizao now. Have you seen General Zuo at all over the past few days?
Things are coming to a head. The Ministry of War is holding meetings day and night, and the general must remain on the front lines to oversee things personally.
Neither he nor I can abandon our posts.
There is truly no way to avoid this conflict?
This isn't something I have the authority to decide.

As an Advisor for the Ministry of War, my job is to do everything in my power to accurately calculate the outcome of the war. Allowing any personal biases to affect my work would be a grave disservice to the people of Yan.
You don't compromise in your duties, and neither do I.
I hope everything goes smoothly...
If the outcome is inevitable, it's best not to worry too much about it.
If you are alone for the Mid-Autumn Festival, come to my home for dinner.
...I will.
Wait... I just thought of something.
Didn't you just ask me who has authority to alter the Academy's records?
That did happen once, although it was under extremely unusual circumstances.
You mean...
No. I can't say that the records were ever altered. However, if a record has gone missing entirely, then it must have been transferred to another location—
Death row, at the Ministry of Justice.
Father is not here?
It's been more than six months since he was last here. The furnishings in the house have not changed, only there are many more symbols symbolizing war on the sand table.
A lone city stands in defiance, the two sides gazing at each other from afar.
Lord Zuo...
A thick, familiar voice rings out from behind.
Uncle Taihe!
Your... your injuries?
I was finally able to walk since two weeks ago, albeit with some difficulty. At least I'm no longer bedridden.
Zuo Le stares at Taihe unbelievably, who does not look like the man he remembers.
The wind lifts a corner of his cloak, revealing his thin, withered figure under his wide robe.
Taihe, once a man so strong and sturdy, is now emaciated. It's as if a mere gust of wind could blow him away.
Uncle Taihe...
Kung fu is merely an external tool. I am, after all, still a monitor censor of the Department of Discipline and Supervision, and there will always be opportunities to realize my aspirations.
If you continue to dwell on this, Lord Zuo, then you're only underestimating my skills.
No...
Taihe, I know you are no ordinary man. A few injuries aren't enough to stop you!
It has been over six months since we last met. You look stronger than before. The General will be pleased.
By the way, Father is...
The General has been in constant meetings with the Sui Regulator and the Ministry of War. He's barely had ten minutes to rest.
The situation...
...Could change in an instant.
Is the court still ready to go to war at all costs?
Now, after the incident in Dahuang, the bestial Sui is no longer under the Sui Regulator's control.
There are some minor disagreements between the cabinet and the Six Ministries, but their overall opinion is clear—
Eliminate the threat once and for all.
But... the consequences of this war will be...
I heard that one month ago, other Candleholders discovered traces of the convict near Baizao.
Yes. I heard the same thing.
The imperial court is on high alert; they've already dispatched several units of Imperial Guards.
The Imperial Guards...
The convict's unpredictability is currently the gravest threat we face.
I have a theory, perhaps...
There is still another Proxy in Baizao...
This Proxy is the 'youngest', and the most unique.
According to the Sui Regulator's observations, in the hundreds of years since he left the tomb, he has never used any of his powers. The Sui Regulator does not know how to deal with him...
There's only one thing that's a little suspicious—one hundred years ago, after Wang escaped from prison, he came to Baizao and has remained here ever since.
Wang used to frequently interact with other Proxies, so I am afraid that during his current return to Baizao, the lone Proxy who stayed here would also be part of his plan.
What do you plan to do?
I don't know yet, so I'm going to go take a look...
...And see for myself.
Phew! Finished!
What do you think? I've memorized every step, from washing the vegetables to serving the noodles. I can do this all by myself now!
Of course, it's all thanks to Xingzhu for figuring out a genius solution—I just need to dip my hands in ice water for five minutes before kneading the dough, and it comes out fine.
It looks good, and I can taste the craftsmanship in the dish.
It'll do, even if it's far from perfect.
If anyone could cook as well as me after just a few classes, then all my years of study would be in vain.
Hey, I've been working hard for so long, and tomorrow is the big day. Can't you at least say something encouraging?
I just don't want you to get a big head... Alright, maybe you're right—a little pep talk might do you good; otherwise, you'd be too scared to cook the minute you meet Mo Bufu.
Remember: there's nothing to be afraid of. Mo Bufu is old. Show him the power of your youth!
Okay!
Everything's ready. Let's go win one at Ding Feng Lou tomorrow!
Welcome to Ding Feng Lou.
Wow... So this is the place everyone's talking about.
Are you... nervous?
Huh? Don't think I should be.
The competition is nigh, and yet, I feel... apprehensive, somehow...
I mean...
Nothing to fear! The Young Chef said it was fine.
Besides, it's not like we're trying to be cooks here. We're just on the hunt for that legendary chef, right? To ask him for his tangerine cake recipe.
Cold comfort, but I suppose at this point that's all I'm getting...
It's about time. All spectators please remain here for now. Competitors, follow me.
If you get anything difficult thrown at you, don't bluster, just keep calm and think. There's always a way...
Relax, okay? I'll see you.
Please, sit.
While I know you're here for the exam, you're still one of our guests. Please, let me offer you some tea first.
The examiner picks up the teapot from the table and pours Blaze some tea. Her nose is immediately filled with the fresh, sweet scent.
For a competition, you're all being pretty friendly here...
Well, can't say I haven't been thirsty. Thanks!
Blaze grabs the teacup and drinks it up.
As she does, the examiner places a pen and paper before her.
This pot of tea is infused with several ingredients. Please write them down.

PFFTH—
*cough* *cough* *hack*... What?!
Blaze glances down at her cup, naught but a bead of tea left on the side. She looks up at the examiner, polite smile still plastered over his face.
Could I, uh... have another cup?
Today looks like a bust to me. They've gone through dozens of people since morning, and nobody's even passed the first stage.
Our head chef is being too harsh on them. It's like a revolving door over there—No one can last longer than a minute.
A chef's third eye is their tongue. If their tongue's not good enough, they can't be that good a chef.
So, no point wasting the head chef's time if they can't even pass stage one. May as well send 'em home sooner.
Hey, hey, 'scuse me, madam, this is the kitchen. Are you—
Judicial Review Office investigation. If I could have both your assistance.
Keep calm... keep calm...
What did Mechanist tell you? The human tongue can only taste four flavors, but the nose can tell between over a hundred...
Not that I ever smelt anything from his 'Machine Oil' moonshine, though...
Blaze gingerly cradles her cooling teacup once more, and takes a careful sniff of what aroma remains.
Smells flowery. That's probably jasmine...
No, not just jasmine, there's a kind of... right, chrysanthemum! Just like the table tea at the Young Chef's place!
Okay, aside from that, there was definitely a sweet-sour kind of flavor as I drank it...
None of the initial flavors were sour, so what was that aftertaste...
Sweet, acid, and a little puckery... kind of familiar?
Why'd you want me to try this plum wine, anyway?
It's a specialty here in Baizao, you see.
There's a plum forest north of the city. When summer sets in June and the plums are fresh and ripe, the people pick them to steep in wine—ideally a Xiaohan wine.
Mmm... wine made from mossoats like this just has a particular flavor to it. It goes perfect with plum.
Steep it for about three months, and it's ready just in time for Mid-Autumn.
Plum wine, huh... So it's got history?
Earlier, you asked me what happened to all the people we lost building Baizao, right?
I wouldn't say it's got history, really. It just strikes me... during those awful times back then, pickled plums were a worker's staple.
And some years after finishing Baizao, that plum forest sprang up in the mountains north of us. No one knows exactly when it did, nor who planted it.
So it's a way of... remembering them, kind of?
At least they were never forgotten.
Once the plums are ripe next year, we can go to the mountains to see together.
Here's my answer.
Jasmine, chrysanthemum, green plum, Mount T'ien blossom, rock sugar... Xiaohan wine?
I've heard there's a plum forest north of Baizao.
The plums in June get brewed for Mid-Autumn. I'm guessing the plums in this were picked earlier than that, which is why there's still a mouth-puckering taste in it.
The alcohol steamed away while boiling the tea, and left just the sweet-sourness of the plum. Plus the mild and subtle aroma from the flower...
It wasn't obvious when I drank it down, but the aftertaste really stays with you... I'd really like another cup!
Identifying the first few flavors is reasonable enough, but you're the first one today who's been able to point out the use of Xiaohan wine.
So... I'm right?
Are you not sure about your own answer?
No! No, of course not! Of course I don't... Haha...
Then congratulations on passing the first stage.
Please, follow me upstairs.
From the rear hall sounds a gong, and the doors next to Blaze yawn open.
First stage...? How long is this thing...?
Here you are, madam. The list of chefs at Ding Feng Lou.
If you don't mind me asking, what are you investigating exactly...?
...The case is confidential. I'm not at leisure to tell you for now.
Okay...
The head chef here, their name is Mo Bufu?
Yes. I don't think there's a cook in Baizao who doesn't know of Chef Mo.
Do you know what his occupation was prior to Ding Feng Lou?

That, I have no idea. Chef Mo arrived... just under ten years ago? Not quite a long time, but not short either. His cooking skills are the real deal, they're sublime. Almost straight away, he succeeded the previous head chef.
Where is he now? I need to see him.
I'm really sorry, but he's holding an exam right this moment. There's no way he can meet you.
Exam? What exam?
You could call it an enrollment exam for Ding Feng Lou? It only happens once every few years.
If you're interested, we're televising it right here!
That contestant...
Blaze...?
This is...
Isn't this a restaurant...? How come this room's set up like a study?
In its centuries of history, Ding Feng Lou has hosted countless reputed celebrities. We set aside this room to safekeep any pieces of calligraphy or painting gifted by them.
Delicacies, calligraphies, paintings... all are works of art that take years of practicing.
Blaze looks up and around. Hundreds upon thousands of silk threads hang from the ceiling, a wooden placard tied to each one, resembling a curtain.
All of these are... names of dishes...

As you're no doubt aware, Ding Feng Lou is hiring chefs in the runup to the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies. This calls for cooks who are not just culinarily skilled, but who grasp the significances behind the dishes they make.
Every dish here originates from a past Feast of a Hundred Delicacies. Pick one placard, and tell me the dish's history. That is today's second stage.
(From the FEASTS? How the hell should I know any of these?!)
(Whatever, I've gotten this far—)
Blaze closes her eyes, and swipes one down. The neighboring placards clatter against each other, as if an endless windchime.
What's this...?
'Medley of Whites'?
Blaze barely knows how to cook anything. Passing the first stage was already a miracle.
She spent basically her whole life in Victoria. How could she possibly know any of these stories...?
Blaze... I really wish I could help you...
You have a time limit. Do you know the origin of this dish?
About fifty years ago, give or take, Yan's Grand Preceptor presented the True Lung with a dish—the simplest, plainest, stewed radishes.
But the special thing was, the radishes she used came from all over the land, and each one had a massively different flavor and texture.
But having been stewed for long enough, they absorbed enough broth and were all equally tasty.

I hear Yan's always put education first, but because the country's so big, and every city has wildly different circumstances anyway, the farther out you go, the harder it is for students to really achieve.
The Grand Preceptor was trying to tell the True Lung that every student, no matter their background, should have equal opportunity.
The True Lung took the Grand Preceptor's advice, advocated hard for educational reform, tore down any regional restrictions and brought equality to educational resources.
The next year, when Baizao Academy announced its successful applicants, students from remote regions made up more than a half of the thousand-plus that qualified.
In the decades after that, the Tianshis buried in their Bureau's books, the students studying like hell in their academies, all of them thanked the Grand Preceptor for boldly advising...
That, if I'm not mistaken, is what Medley of Whites represents...
Blaze senses the spectators stop chattering.
She's not wrong. Everyone is nervously watching her right this moment.
(How... did Blaze know that?)
(And why would the exam writer leave the name of this dish in there...?)
(You don't just mention her, not in front of everyone...)
Did I, uh, say something wrong...?
Interesting.
The voice descends from a few meters above. Blaze stares up above her, but doesn't see anyone.
A foot lands on a stairstep plank with a dull thud, subdued yet powerful, the kind nobody could miss.
It approaches, closer and closer, until Blaze can finally make out who it is—a stern and solemn man, far older than her, alighting from his long stairwell.
After so many years, I didn't think anyone would remember this.
Nice to meet you, um...
The story you've told is far older than you. Girl, how come you know it?
Well, someone from my family told me it, a long long time ago.
He said he was one of those students in poverty, all the way back then.
To know the spring of your water... I imagine your relative was a grateful and righteous man.
But as for you...
Just what motive brought you here?
I... well, I'm... here to compete, of course...
Seeing as you've made it here, I'll give you one chance.
Final stage. No frills, no fuss. Make me a 'dish'.
Come.
Too early for dinnertime, the restaurant is almost empty save for a couple of diners. There's no waiters, no proprietor, and even the kitchen is quiet.
Excuse me...
You, young man! Help me out!
I was in a hurry this morning and forgot my money. You're a well-to-do boy, anyone can tell! Be a kind man, spot me a little!
Er...
I'm not here to rip you off. Here, take this family jade for now. Once I grab the money later, I'll pay you back.
Not that I mind lending you... but couldn't you just tell the owner instead...? We're total strangers—how would you find me and pay me back?
No, no, no, you see, this jade's not your average thing, it's not fit for just anyone to take. I'm only entrusting it to you because I can tell, you've got a rare air about you.
A-Alright...
Zuo Le rummages for some loose coins in his pocket, and hands them to the diner, whose face immediately broadens with delight.
When do you plan to pay me back...? Where would we meet?
We'll meet if we do and we won't if we don't. And if we don't, you can just go ahead and pawn this jade off.
Relax, relax. You've made a favorable deal today, young sir.
So saying, the diner is gone out the restaurant door in a blink, disappearing into the crowds.
Pawn... it off?
There's my dish. Pretty good job, wouldn't you say?
...Longevity noodles?
Mo Bufu's hands rest on the table, chopsticks in his grasp. For a while, silence, before he finally picks up the spoon and tries a mouthful of soup.
Blaze examines Chef Mo's face closely, but the satisfied look she's hoping for simply does not blossom across the old man's face.
These noodles... this soup base, who taught you this?
It was this tiny little chef. I really put my back into learning it, too...
The owner of 'Savors at Yu's'. Who is he to you?
How come you know...?
I thought so...
...The disgrace of him!
Huh...?
I see he has talent and give him the chance to display his skills. He's not grateful for that, fine. But now, now he even sends an amateur to humiliate me!
What's happening? Blaze finished it up with no trouble... nothing should be wrong with the flavor.
...Does Mo Bufu know the Young Chef somehow?
Wait! Okay, hold on!
I'm sorry! I know you can probably tell I suck at cooking at a glance, but I didn't come so I could wreck your competition!
I just have a question that only you can answer!
I grew up in Victoria, see, and this relative of mine who lived far away in Yan would always send me these tangerine cakes. They were so sweet and tasty...
I've been on the hunt ever since I came to Baizao, but I still haven't found that flavor...
I was thinking, since you're the best chef in all of Baizao, maybe you would know!
A single pastry? You thought it was worth entering this competition to meet me, over a single pastry?
I have tons of other questions still! But this is the only one I can solve on my own. This is the one clue, the one link I have to my family!
You...
...The greatest taboo when making tangerine cakes is to blindly add sugar. That is, to mask the poorness of your craft and ingredients with sweetness.
I don't know of anyone skilled enough to make tangerine cakes that are sweet and yet taste good on their own.
You've asked your question. Now go.
Wait—
A muffled sound from above interrupts the two. Blaze and Mo Bufu look up at the same time, but both of their faces get struck by something wet.
Why is it leaking in here...?
No... this is oil!
And the moment she says it, the lamp suspended from the dome ignites in a blink, becoming a fireball—
Get outta here!
Watch out!
What even—
Flames burst forth above their heads, raining cinders down from the ceiling, igniting the flour and oil atop the stove. In an instant, the kitchen is a sea of fire.
*cough* *cough* *cough*!
You... go... I'll be fine!
Cover your mouth and nose! Don't talk!
I'm not too good with this move yet... let's give it a try.
'Scuse me, just duck.
Blaze grabs a knife, then slices her palm open. Gradually a whirlwind forms in Blaze's hand, then expands straight outward into a blast wave.
The violent surge of air sweeps the whole room. In a blink, she's put the fires out, and strewn the cookware about in shambles.
What a skill you have... Could you be...
...Duoxie.

You're welcome—
Hm?
Looking up again, Blaze sees the lamp's frame is charred black, red-hot metal peeking through the burnt coating. It's on the verge of falling.
Mo Bufu, move!
Blaze grabs the old man's arm, trying to pull him to one side, but the lamp's already falling—
With a stifled crack of thunder, the remains of the fixture are abruptly flung to one side, and smashed hard against the wall.
Are you alright there?
Leizi?! What are YOU doing here?
...Maybe I should ask, what are you doing here?
But we can talk about you later...
Could this be a repeat of the Ding Feng Lou fire last month...?
Lin Qingyan scans her surroundings. The kitchen chefs are in a frenzied jumble, but her gaze quickly locks onto a masked figure among them.
...Who seems to meet her gaze, then turns and vanishes into the crowd.
Blaze, stay here! Keep Mo Bufu safe!
Hey! Where are you going?
*cough* *cough*... Jeez, this smoke is killing me.
*cough* *hack*... On that pillar there, there's a breaker...
There's, uh... a lot of them here... Which one do I pull...?
Flitting like a fowl, Lin Qingyan has her target cornered in just a few spry leaps.
Mere meters separate them, but so smoky is the air that she can't quite make out their silhouette.
Just as he makes to run again, a thunderclap strikes next to his feet.
Hold it!
Move another inch, and the next bolt is hitting your head.
Answer my questions.
Why set fire to Ding Feng Lou? Is your target Mo Bufu, or someone else?
All the previous incidents, they were your doing too, weren't they?
Also... the scrollery?
You... and your accomplices, must have some aim in all these actions—what is it?
Not talking? Then I'll take you back to the Judicial Review Office, for a nice long questioning—
Deputy Minister Lin, surely you wouldn't ask if you didn't know the answer already?
Can you not see the truth, or do you simply pretend you can't because you don't dare to?
What—
The Office is a good place, but I don't plan on visiting it for now.
Besides, when your will wavers, the thunder you throw isn't so frightening.
It's time you opened your eyes.
Wait—
And with that, the masked figure is on the move. Just as Lin Qingyan would give chase, the ceiling sprinklers start up out of nowhere.
Ice-cold water pours down, soaking her head to foot.
Leizi—
You alright?
Dammit.
The customers are all safely out of the way, there's just a few in shock. Thank goodness no one was injured...
We've reported it to the authorities. The mandarins should be here any moment...
Don't we have a mandarin here already?
Head Chef... you're...
I'm fine... I owe it to this girl.
There's nothing left for you to do here. Go on, go home.
Once the chefs leave, Lin Qingyan locks the door to the room.
Mr. Mo, if I could have a word or two with you.
You know this girl, madam?
She's a friend of mine.
Chef Mo, she's on the trail of a really important case. Would it be alright if you, uh, tried to answer her questions...?
Hmph... I don't like what the mandarins get up to, but I don't like to spurn a favor either. You saved my life today, and that's more favor than I'll ever repay.
Go ahead, then. Ask away.
I'm looking for one of your restaurant's chefs. He's a witness in a case.
I believe ten years ago, he was working in the Ministry of Justice death row prison, making last meals for convicts.
A chef who only makes last meals... How exactly did he pass the Ding Feng Lou exams?
Ding Feng Lou serves all manner of mandarins every year at the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies. That means every chef's background must be clear as day for the record.
I imagine he shouldn't be hard to find.
What exactly do you want me to say, madam?
First my restaurant catches fire for no reason. Now you say we have a chef who made meals for dead men. Should I even bother to open Ding Feng Lou again?
I understand your situation, sir. That's why I'm choosing to ask you these things in private.
Whatever. It's not like I can't admit to my own past.
The chef you're looking for, the death row caterer Mo Xiaoliu, that was me.
What?!
I thought so...
And making last meals is nothing shameful. The powerful need to eat, and so do the powerless.
My cooking skills come from that prison, making last meals for death row convicts from all corners of the country.
As for changing my name and hiding my identity, I just didn't want to make any trouble for this restaurant.
I'm beginning to... sort of admire you.
Do you remember, ten years ago, a convict admitted to the prison by the name of Gu Quan?
If you don't remember the name, you should at least remember that some decades ago, he was the one and only person to be released from death row.
What?!
Wait! You mean my dad? Why'd my dad get the death penalty?
Blaze!
You mean Gu Quan? He was your father?
Yeah...
So you remember him...
I do... all too well.

I spent so many years in there making last meals. I met countless horrific scoundrels, endless connivers. As each one of them ate before they left, the look in their eyes, it was always more or less the same.
But I'd never seen someone whose eyes were so clear, so clean, so free of anything.
He wasn't like the other convicts. In his time, he never wept, never howled, and never screamed injustice, not even on the day he was imprisoned.
Even I found it strange how a man like him could end up on death row.
Why would... my dad...
Then do you know, at the time, what charges Gu Quan was sentenced to death on?
Not at the time...
Not at the time?
But one year later, when I joined Ding Feng Lou and began taking part in the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies, I came across a menu for a Feast from a few years before—
That was when I learned that at that Feast, Gu Quan served a dish to the True Lung. It just so happens, that was the same dish this girl pulled down earlier. 'Medley of Whites'.
But next to that dish, he placed a steamed flour bun, and in it... was a filling of red pickled tofu.
So... what does it mean? Why would he get locked up just for serving that?
Lin Qingyan, aren't you gonna say anything?
Your relative told you the story of the Grand Preceptor, but never told you her eventual fate?
Her... fate?

Forty years ago, the Grand Preceptor plotted to assassinate the True Lung. She committed suicide after it failed. Every single one of her underlings was arrested and trialed, and their numbers were over a thousand...
When Gu Quan served that dish at the Feast, it signified redressing her for her treason. It made the True Lung furious.
No way... my dad told me so many times, the Grand Preceptor was loyal to the country, the people, the whole realm! How the hell could she be a traitor?!
The history books have it that way, yes.
And they're right?!
Lin Qingyan, if everything written in them is true, what the hell are you investigating?!
And... and besides, my dad didn't actually get killed, right? Doesn't that prove, like, at least some people from the court believed the Grand Preceptor and my dad were both innocent?!
That's a sticking point, yes. If Gu Quan was imprisoned as part of the Grand Preceptor case, why would they free him afterwards?
It stuck with me too... You see, after entering prison, Gu Quan had two visitors, and both were people I happened to know...
One was the person delivering his meal. Yu Cheng, deputy minister of the Office at the time.
And the other was the one who brought him out... former Minister of Rites, Ning Shu.
What...?!
Quick! This way! Surround the place immediately!
Dammit... why now...?!
What a coincidence. One step ahead of me, Deputy Minister Lin.
I'd just arrived. A coincidence, indeed.
The Judicial Review Office is investigating the Ding Feng Lou fire. I ask all of you to be cooperative.
Lin Qingyan, and the girl next to you, come with me.

lines.st: Let someone better take over? Haha, if Father heard that, I'm sure he'd be chewing your head off.
Don't joke with me, Brother. Of all us siblings, who could compare to you in either knowledge or strength?

Once you ascend the throne and move into the Forbidden City, I can leave for the Sky Mirror Pavilion with peace of mind. I'll set up shop as a scribe and look after all Yan's ancient records. In a way, I'll also take some weight off your shoulders.
Brother, no more hesitating! At this point, only you can... convince Father.
The Grand Preceptor... no, all three Grand Ministers support this move! Even in the face of the officers, of Yan's citizens, public opinion will still bear out.
Brother, this is the right thing to do! You can't pull out now!
...I'll stand with you.
Why me? Why did it have to be me?!
Our youngest brother died because of you! They all died because of you!
You shove it all on me, make me take the blame alone, deal with the whole warring mess alone, and you just leave like nothing happened?!
Yan Wu, never return again.

lines.x: Look how idle you are. All the Mid-Autumn festivities of Lungmen, and you don't plan to attend a single one?
Should I? Who wants to see my wrinkly mug on their TV, anyway?
The Schwires' girl is a planner. She'll handle it. She can make anything lively.

I've watched you grow lazier day after day since we came back from Yumen. You just coop up in your study at home eating snacks. If you don't go out for a walk any time soon, you might just start putting on a beer belly.
Hah hah... I haven't seen Lin Kojui get out much since spring. I can't even find a partner for morning jogs now.
Ah... he's old, I'm old, the young ones know how to do it all. If I keep sticking my head in, they won't think I'm cool.
So now you understand, huh? If you'd realized any sooner, would little Ch'en have ever left?
How long ago was it?
Sorry, what?
Little Ch'en. How long has it been since she left?
Five years.
It's been five years.
We don't even know what she's going through out there, or how her disease is.
I know she was at Yumen, when I was there.
She's doing fine. Her swordsmanship's progressed... so she's fine, I suppose.
So you just watched her leave again?
She doesn't want to see me, and she doesn't want to come back. I can't force her.
Don't worry. Her path's longer than ours.
Lord Wei, a letter to you.
...That they made you deliver personally...
It's from the capital.
The sender...?
His Majesty's Imperial Guard.
Did he have any message aside from the letter?
The True Lung invites Lord Wei to the capital for the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies, and for a moon-viewing together.
Leave the letter on my desk. You're dismissed for now.
If we may ask what Lord Wei intends.
I have my own plans. None of you need to worry.
Lord Wei, we volunteer to escort you to the capital!
...I said you're dismissed!
Why don't we open up your invitation and see what they wrote exactly?
No need. Just leave it there.
This day was meant to come. I even thought it'd come a little sooner.
...How many years has it been?
Forty... it's been forty whole years since I left Baizao.
Yumen should've reached the capital by now. I suppose my brother's settling all his old debts in one clean go.
A clean slate in time for autumn... A fine autumn at that.
You can choose not to go.
It's been forty years already, what's he going to do? Send another unit of Imperial Guards, a whole army—
...Enough nonsense!
At this point, that's not what he intends...
Then what does he intend?
You know, I've never really brought up those days with him before...
Enough. I don't want to hear it.
Fumizuki, I've let down a lot of people throughout my life...
Edward, my sister, my teacher...
And I have to admit that my brother's turned out to be one more on the pile.
You can also say that it was the position that killed so many people.
...And who had a choice?
Then it wasn't your fault.
Even if you'd stayed, would things have turned out better? Would there be no blood?
Nobody would have remembered a minister pleading the commoners' case. They would only remember a regicidal, fratricidal prince!
If you have questions, ask. I'm in a hurry.
Mind your attitude. You're also a theoretical suspect in our crime scene. But seeing as you're a coworker, I'll spare you the interrogation room.
I told you! I saw the culprit on the scene, I just miscalculated and failed to arrest him—
Calm down. Are you going to physically vent on me just because you didn't catch the one who did it?
...I'd have to match your strength first.
Good. You're still somewhat sober.
In light of the years we've spent together in the Judicial, then, can you tell me—
This afternoon, why were you at Ding Feng Lou, at the scene of the crime?
...An investigation.
In the runup to the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies, we've had the scrollery be burned, then two fires at Ding Feng Lou. Don't you think this string of cases could be connected somehow?
So you're saying you knew beforehand that someone would set fire to Ding Feng Lou today, and thus you were lying in wait for them?
I was just there following up a lead...
'Just' there following up a lead... Do you need me to list off every action you've taken the last few days?
You've been tailing me?
I don't have that sort of free time... but I have ways of knowing these things, naturally.
Let me ask once more. Who were you meeting at Ding Feng Lou today?
...I fear I'm not the only one working on the sly.
Explain?

For your information, you never told me the Ministry of Rites' files on Gu Quan were forged, and that he was sent to death row before leaving Baizao... As a minister, you must have known these things.
These are all clearly key points of evidence. Why were they nowhere to be found in the Judicial Review Office archives? And why would you cover up—
So this is the lead on the scrollery fire that you've been toiling over for the last few days...? How did you find any of this out?
It's none of your concern how I found out...
There is a piece of advice I gave to Yu Cheng in the past, and I will give it to you now.
Some past affairs are learned of. Some are not learned of. In both cases, there are reasons why.
Some things are best left forgotten by all.
I never imagined I'd hear those words come from a minister.
You may consider me a schemer all you like, a minister merely booted over from the Ministry of Justice.
But I know exactly what I'm saying.
And I know that regardless of the thousands of reasons you might have, the truth is still and merely the truth, not anyone's tool to broaden the divide.
There will always be someone who remembers the truth, and the meaning behind it.
For example, that girl shut in the next room—whom you took every pain to secretly bring back to Baizao all the way from Victoria?
Yes... I did uncover that Gu Quan had a daughter in Victoria. She too can provide vital testimony in the Gu Quan case—
Lin Qingyan, when will you stop playing dumb?
You've already found out that the Gu Quan files are fake. You know Yu Cheng is on the trail of that very case...

So I'm guessing you've also found out about the child who the Imperial Guard brought back forty years ago, who they recorded in the files as deceased... You have your suspicions as to the truth of this case, correct?
Was that girl you brought back here born in Victoria, or born in Baizao?
You know full well.
You clearly long suspected her identity, and still, you decided to bring her back. Were you that confident you could pull this off without a hitch...
Or was it that in pursuit of the 'truth', you didn't even care if she lived or died?
You're threatening me...
You think I'm joking?
Lin Qingyan's gaze fixes tight on the minister's notes. She doesn't even notice how her own attire is soaked through in cold sweat.
But in the next instant, with a bizarre warping of light, the paper in her hands vanishes.
But back on topic, that's not what I care about. At least, right now, it's not what I care about most.
I've told you, past affairs are never as important as present.
So what are these present affairs of yours?
You know that Yumen is less than a hundred li from Baizao right now. For three months, Baizao has been preparing to split up and move.
...And how does this relate to the Gu Quan case?
Should you keep investigating this case, then there will only be one person in all of Yan who can give you your answers.
Qingyan, take one step out of this door, and I can no longer protect you.
...Let alone her.
At the Ch'ens' old place, in the study, there's a secret closet. After I leave, go there, and take what's inside.
Follow those instructions, and you can protect Lungmen's safety, and your own.
So you're going?
Now's the perfect time to. Kojui's girl is doing a great job handling the downtown, and the Schwires' future doesn't rest on the sick old tiger anymore.
Lungmen doesn't need a Wei Yenwu anymore, or a Chief Executive...
I think you're giving your last words a little too early.
This is about Lungmen's future... Fumizuki, listen to me...
I've made a lot of mistakes, done a lot of things I regret... but Lungmen, our city, built by us... a home to millions—
Lungmen must stay.
Then you stay too, and take care of it.
Youngsters can handle it all, but it still needs some oldies to look after it.
You see this letter too. How am I meant to ignore this?
So once again you'll just roll over and die? Just like last time you'd pay with your life, is that how you're envisioning it? Does that make anything even?
...It's different this time!
There's no one standing on the other side, I'm just facing my own debts.
I made a choice forty years ago... and ever since, with every action I take, I try to convince myself—that even knowing how many people I've failed, I was still right to make that choice.
But I can't run forever.
You know, your life's never been yours alone. Not then, and certainly not now.
Forget about sacrificing yourself to make up for anything. You can't make up for a mistake with another mistake—it never ends that way. At our age, we should've already learned that lesson well.
Stay. We'll face this together.
Fumizuki... Don't try and stop me!
You cannot stop me... paying for my past.
Well then, you leave me with no other choice.
While I'm stood here, you're not going anywhere.
Yenwu, this time, I'll protect you. I swear it.
Leizi!
You're... you're alright?
Of course I'm alright!
All that Judicial lady asked was what I saw during the fire, and I just told the truth—I didn't see anything! So then she let me go.
The heck did you do? How come you took so long?
It's no matter now. Let's go.
Wait, wait, wait! Go? Why?
That Mo Bufu clearly still has tons of answers! Aren't we gonna follow up with him?!
There's nothing to follow up on.
Pardon?
I'm not meddling with your father's case anymore.
I'll get you out of Baizao in the next day or two.
Lin Qingyan! That's one stupid joke to tell me now, you know that?!
No, wait... you just found a new lead, didn't you? That's why you're just screwing with me?
Okay now, for real, tell me what you found or—
Blaze!
You—
I got the official debriefing from the Judicial case files.
Gu Quan... an ethnic Yanese from Victoria, joined the Ministry of Rites in 1077 as a ceremonial officer.
Reassigned as envoy of ceremonial rites in 1092, headed for Victoria as part of a diplomatic mission. On the way, encountered an unexpected snowstorm, and passed away after falling into a river.
What...?
Leizi... you're lying to me, aren't you...? Just trying to shove me out of the picture again?
You've been asking me just what the deal was with your father's death, haven't you?
Well, every lead's been thoroughly investigated. There's no doubt. That's it, it was just an accident...
All the gaps in the files have been made replete.
...This case can be closed.
In creeps the mid-autumn. The night sky is moonless.
Out in the streets, the energy poles churn away at max output, but the drop in temperature is still noticeable to some.
All falls still in the dead of night. The human sea recedes. The biting breeze blows through.
Achoo—!
If you're that cold, just turn on the heater.
Easy for you to say. If we're going to keep running this shabby little eatery, we have to pinch every penny we can on warmth...
Dammit, look how late it is. When will Blaze and her friend show up?
You're wondering about that reward money, huh...
A... Am not! Achoo—!
Is this window letting air in? I need to fix it later—
