Wait, are they back...?
You... You're—
What? Am I not allowed in?
P-Please, come inside, I'll put on some tea for you now... Anything to eat?
Noodles. Longevity noodles.
R... Right, I'll make you some now!
Ones made by your boss.
W-Well...
Jiang glances back at the staircase where the Young Chef had been standing; by now he's vanished without a trace.
Er... he's out on business.
Then I'll wait for him here.
Far, far out... he won't be back for a fortnight.
And I'll still wait.
I've waited thirty years. So long that I'm shriveled up. What's a little longer?
Chef Mo, please don't joke around now...
You're the first under heaven when it comes to cooking. What could be worth wasting your time here?
Ma de... First under whose heaven?!
Mo Bufu downs his teacup in one go.
The hot tea spills from the corners of his mouth, and drips down his wizened white beard.

I remember clearly... that winter thirty years ago, a Catastrophe wrecked my home village. Those of us lucky enough to survive took off, me with nothing but my cooking skills. We thought, let's scrape by in Baizao.
We went through hell. We were almost there when we ran into another group of refugees—or maybe a bandit gang, I'm not sure.
They followed us for a long way across that icy waste, weapons in hand. We began to realize that they were after the remaining supplies and rations we had left.
By the time we'd been trekking for a month, plenty of us were sick or hurt. We had barely scraps left to eat, far from enough for both sides to cross that wasteland.
There was no escape, so we decided we may as well risk it all with them. However, when we were just about to go out fighting, we smelt something delicious. That's when we saw him...
There was an old temple not far away from where we were. Someone'd set up a stove by the gateway, boiling up a wok of noodles. We couldn't see his face properly, through all the steam.
We had no clue why someone would be making noodles there, but we were past the point of wondering, of asking, even of fighting.
That wonderful smell and those piping hot noodles drew all of us near the wok, and we waited around him quietly.
Never before had I eaten something so delicious...
The moment those noodles entered my mouth, I felt like my tongue had been numb for decades, only to spring back to life right then and there.
All along, I never knew that a meal could taste so arresting, so absorbing.
It felt like a flavor beyond us humans...
By the time I finished my noodles and looked up from my bowl, he was gone.
Who knew that in my lifetime studying as a cook, the most profound lesson I'd learn would be in a shack of a temple outside Baizao City.
From then on, I apprenticed all over, so that one day, I might make a bowl of noodles like those.
But I couldn't... In the end, I just can't.
You know, what if you're misremembering... I mean, look, you were starving at the time, maybe that's why you found that bowl of noodles so tasty—
Do you not trust my tongue?
Thirty years a bowl of noodles has been burned into my mind. Thirty years I've been searching for it.
If whoever that was is still in Baizao, he must be over fifty by now...
I searched all over this city. The well-known tea maker in the north, nothing. The guy who made the best pastries in the south, still nothing...
I searched and searched, until my fellow oldies in cooking retired, and only I was left. I was on the verge of giving up...
But today, once more, I tasted it...
I don't know why... her cooking skills were immature, but the noodles she brought forth tasted exactly like it.
It was your chef here who taught her those noodles, am I right?
How do you know...
We sent out a hundred written invitations for the Ding Feng Lou competition. I can recall every one. I've long been aware your Savors at Yu's had a reputation.
I don't know what kind of relation your chef and that savior of mine have, and I have no clue why he's willing to waste his talents away like this.
But I won't do the same... All the cooking skills I've inherited over the years, I can't let them end with me.
Mo Bufu stands, produces a letter from his pocket, and places it on the table.
...You tell him this. That untrained disciple of his? She passes.
Tell her to report to Ding Feng Lou two days later before noon.
Huh...? She just—passes?
You get good enough business here. Did this doorstep sink again recently?
We haven't replaced it in over ten years.
Low doorsteps can't keep the wind out. It's getting colder in the coming days.
The boss won't let me. He says the lower, the better. Too high and all the diners will stop coming.
Mo Bufu steps over that low, low doorstep, and disappears into the thick of night.
Once again, the restaurant is empty save for squalls of wind through the hall. Their whistle grows louder, as if lamenting.
...Is he gone?
*Sigh*... You really are—*Sigh*!
I heard Blaze passed the test? Does that mean the prize money—
Forget about the prize money!
What's wrong with you to torment a man like that... He's seventy, eighty years old, and you'll just watch him freak out like that?
He just can't find an apprentice to fit him, right? What's to worry about... Look at me, I never worry about things like that.
Put yourself in his shoes, he's spent a lifetime honing his craft, just for it to be lost in a blink—
Well, if it's lost, it's lost.
Ai—!

It's his future reputation he cares about right now, it's the title of 'first under heaven' he's carrying, it's others' cuisine he's obsessed with—there's no way he can focus on his own craft and improve it at this rate.
If he can't make it through this, nobody can help him.
Aiyo, Blaze's back.
Look how late it is, I bet you haven't eaten? I'll go make you something now.
Yeah...
Congrats! I've got good news for you, you passed the Ding Feng Lou exam.
We'll split the reward money thirty-seventy like we agreed—
Yeah.
You don't care?
If you're that good for money, how about leaving your thirty percent of—
Huh? Xingzhu didn't come back with you?
Right, where's Xingzhu?
Oh, she dropped in this afternoon while Jiang was out buying ingredients. She left you a note too.
Left me a note...?
You have passed the Ding Feng Lou Chef Selection. Please fol—
Oh, whoops, whoops, wrong one. It's this one.
What could be so secret she has to leave it in a note for you?
She's... inviting me to her home.
...Ning Manor?
You came.
Of course I did. That's what you do when the Nings invite you.
...I'm sorry, I didn't mean to hide anything from you. I just... don't really think it's something I should bring up for no reason...
Nothing to apologize for. You've got your troubles. I'm not the kind to give you the stink eye just for being blue-blooded.
Sooo, what do I call you, lady of House Ning?
My name is... Ning Yin.
I'm... not actually part of House Ning. I was adopted by Grandfather as a child.
He always introduced me as a distant relative of the Nings, but I've never met any 'relative' who actually shares blood with me.
Do you remember what I told you before?
Look, I'm pretty bad at comforting people...
No, it's okay. Grandfather's very nice to me, and my background has never caused me any trouble at all... everyone treats me very well.
Your grandpa would be... Ning Shu? Ex-Minister of Rites?
Yes...
Perfect, I—
I know... I know.
It's getting chilly. Don't just stand out there, let's go inside and talk. Okay?
Sure, I guess.
The corridor meanders on, through a rock garden and a bamboo thicket, then between the two halves of a lotus pond.

A cool breeze brushes through; the autumn waters softly ripple, and in them Blaze's reflection follows behind Ning Yin's as they pace across the bridge. The two pass through a deserted room, packed-up belongings and dead leaves strewn everywhere.
The courtyard is messy, I know. Not presentable to guests, at the very least. I apologize.
The whole family's moving out of here soon. Grandfather can't take Baizao's dry climate in his old age. We're planning to move back to the old place in Jiangnan.
Though Ning Yin's pace is glacial, she can only hurry up once she notices her friend in no mood to appreciate the scenery.
Up ahead. We're almost there.
Take a seat. I'll fetch some tea and refreshments from the kitchen. Our house cooks are all on leave, so I can't serve you much more than some cold sides—
No need to bother. I can't stick around long.
...Are things that urgent?
Well, alright...
Ning softly closes the door. The chirps of fowl outside fade. The room grows heavier with silence.
Alright. Tell me why you invited me here.
I'm sorry... I was going to look for you yesterday, actually, after the fire at Ding Feng Lou.
But I overheard your conversation with the Deputy Minister of Judicial Review Office and Chef Mo...
And?
Blaze, I... might have met your father before.
When I was fifteen years old, I remember we had a visitor.
Grandfather was always busy with work. His apprentices and coworkers were coming and going in the estate. None of my business, by and large.
But slowly, I realized an extra person had moved in, wearing blue low-ranking mandarin's robes. Every time I saw him, he was alone, as if other people just weren't his concern.
At first, I didn't really pay attention. It was later that year, on my birthday, when...
Like every other year, Grandfather would hold this great big birthday dinner for me.
With lights and banners strung up everywhere, and friends and family all around... not that I knew more than a handful of them.
They were chatting the day away, and about what, I had no clue. So eventually I sneaked out of the dinner, and nobody noticed when I did.
I wandered around the courtyard for ages, bored out of my mind, then scampered into Grandfather's study on a whim.
It was in there where I saw the man again.
I remember I saw him stuffing his pocket with letter papers and brushes. When he noticed me, his face was stretched with nervousness, and he pulled the brush and paper back out again in a rush.
Are you...
It's not what it looks like!
I... needed to finish copying some letters, so I was going to take them home. The High Minister is sending these to very important mandarins, so I was just worried if the paper wasn't good enough...
I just...
It's okay, it's just brushes and paper. You're welcome to make use of it, Sir.
Thank you, little lady.
Um, what's your name...?
My name is... Gu Quan. I'm a transcriber for your estate. I copy and file the estate correspondence.
I'm Ning Yin...
Miss Ning... Of course, I know you.
Then now we know each other.
Sir, I want to read in here for a bit. I won't interrupt you while you work. Is that okay?
O—Of course...! Feel free, Miss Ning.
Mm-hm.
Miss Ning... are you into these history books?
Mm-hm. Grandfather always says one should view history as a mirror. We must use it to study our ancestors' wisdom and character, and learn from their teachings.
But I think reading's just fun. It's like all these amazing people recorded in the texts are coming to life before me, and they're all my friends.
I think someday, it might be nice to join the Imperial Historical Cabinet as a historian myself.
You've got quite a unique outlook, Miss Ning...
If you're interested in following in the High Minister's family footsteps, I'm sure you'd do well.
You think Grandfather's really cool too, then?
...Of course. Minister Ning's been helming the Ministry of Rites for decades. He has plenty of deeds to his name. He's the mandarin everyone loves.
Mm-hm... everyone in this family is so cool.
My uncle and aunt are always away from Baizao, traveling all over the country. I hear Yan couldn't have set up the meteorological and earthquake systems it has today without them.
And Ciqiu, my little sister, she's even cooler.

She's two years younger than me, but at the last Baizao Academy study camp, all the professors from the college of foreign affairs were praising her! Everyone was teasing her by calling her 'Little Miss Minister'!
All the people in my family are going to leave their names in the history books.
I love them and respect them a lot. I want to record who they are, and everything they do... but I could never become like them.
Compared to them, I'm just... normal.
You know, that reminds me of something my master once said to me.
'As history has ebbed and flowed, the floods and tides are recorded, yet most are streams that simply trickle on by... But without the babbling brook, how would the waves arise?'
The waves and brooks don't care about who's greater, or who's plainer...
Indeed, the vast majority will never get to leave their names in the history annals, but they're not the empty space between the lines. They're the pages themselves.
I'm sorry, I talked your ear off there...
Sir, your handwriting is really beautiful.
Oh, well, this... it's my only skill, really. I can still remember when the High Minister gave me this transcriber job.
I can never write my name properly. Grandfather's gotten on my case about it more times than I can remember.
Handwriting is a long road. Just keep at it, and you'll see change eventually.
Maybe you could be my calligraphy teacher, then?
I would never pose as your teacher with my modest brushwork, Miss. But...
Right... I have two copybooks here. I copy from them all the time myself. The forms are very regular—it's perfect for beginners.
If you don't mind, Miss Ning, you can... you can have these as your birthday present.
How did you know my birthday's today?
Well, they decorate the estate so brightly this time every year, you see. I've spent long enough here that I've heard.
Miss Ning... Happy birthday.
Th—Thank you.
That conversation always stayed with me. I liked how he talked, and how he saw things.
And I don't know why—I can't really say—but he felt so close to me, in a way.
But after that, I saw him much less around the estate. The next time I got to talk to him, it was years and years later.

Grandfather's cold has lasted for a month by now, and it's still not getting any better. Now it's almost winter—it might only get worse at this rate. We'll be counting on you for the next while, doctor.
The High Minister's malady is mostly from overwork. You could remind him to rest more often, my lady...

Mm-hm, I will—
Ah.
Sorry! I'm sorry...
I apologize for running into you, Miss.
*cough* *cough*... *cough* *hack*—Very... very sorry...
It's—it's alright! Sir... are you okay?
Why don't you sit down and rest for a while? You really look... quite ill.
I'm fine... I'm fine.
Sir, you dropped your—sir?
Are these... painkillers?
*hiss*... Strong painkillers at that... Whoever that was, he must be quite badly ill.
Doctor, if you're free, could I ask you to attend to that man too? You can bill his medical expenses to me.
I was worried about Grandfather's condition at the time, and didn't pay that encounter much mind—I was just planning to pay him a visit later at some point, when I had the time.
But after that winter, I never saw him again.
Doctor, thank you so much for all your work. Grandfather's back in perfect health now.
Right. And about the man I asked you to attend to...
Oh, you mean that Gu Quan man.
He died.
Was it his illness...?
No... I hear some kind of accident took him while he was on his way to Victoria.
But given his health, I can't imagine he'd have lived much longer even then...
Truth is, last I met him, his malady had taken root in his vitals—I could hear it in his breathing. I can't imagine how much he was suffering in life...
And it's a mystery to me why he'd decide to travel so far while that sick...
This... very room. It was the study Sir Gu Quan was using.
It's been ten years since he passed away. The place has gone through some changes in that time.
But...
This...
I couldn't tell you why... but I kept hold of this medicine bottle ever since.
Blaze... I really am so sorry...
He was—my dad was nothing like that...
I don't think he was ever that fighting fit, but he was never weak. Whenever I was with him, he always had this light in his eyes—
How could...
Even now, I'm not sure I understand Sir Gu Quan... but I know he was a warm, gentle person. I could tell.
I blamed myself over and over, for not being able to do anything more for him...
Why are you bothering to... tell me all this...?
I know you suspect that my grandfather was somehow behind your father's passing. At the point, I can only tell you what I know, but—
You told me all of this just to try and convince me Ning Shu is INNOCENT?!
N—No.
I just wanted to implore you... please, find out the truth.
Whether for Sir Gu Quan's sake... or for the sake of the truth itself.
Whatever that truth—even is...
Are you...
You don't have to tell me twice... I am gonna get to the bottom of this.
What first struck Lin Qingyan about the Judicial Review Office was the solemn hush.
The first time she ever set foot through this courtroom's door, all noise promptly vanished behind her. She remembers Yu Cheng telling her why it was designed so.
Silence begets peace, peace begets thought, thought begets results.
Shouldn't the Judicial Review Office be drowning in work top to bottom? And yet you've got the office all to yourself, Deputy Minister Lin?
Jie Zhen...? What are you here for?
Censorate routine investigation. There are some issues we'd like to consult you on regarding the Yu Cheng case.
Consultation or investigation, which is it?
Deputy Minister Lin, after working together for years, I implicitly trust you on a personal level.
But the Department of Discipline and Supervision has a lot riding on this case. We have to go through the motions, Lin, if you'll pardon me.
Just... ask what you need to.
Name.
Lin Qingyan.
Gender.
...Female.
Um... let's skip the basic info for now. For the next few questions, you don't need to elaborate on your answers, just respond 'yes' or 'no'.
Are you grilling me?
Everything you say from hereon will be recorded. I'm worried what you say could fly against you.
If you have no further questions, let's begin.
Deputy Minister Lin, you passed Tianshi Bureau assessments in childhood and were exceptionally admitted per your outstanding grades, and followed the esteemed Cyanstorm Count in learning your Lei Fa.
After graduating from the Tianshi Bureaus, you chose to join the Judicial Review Office, and soon accompanied then-Deputy Minister Yu Cheng in investigations all across the realm.
In just a year, from Registrar, you rose several ranks to become the youngest Deputy Minister in the history of Yan.
Is this background, as stated, true?
Yes.

In your third month with the Judicial Review Office, as Registrar, you handed in a whistleblower report to Discipline and Supervision. It accused the Right Assistant Revenue Minister of embezzlement and extortion, and you recovered ten million in stolen assets.

In your fifth month, in Changji, you essentially singlehandedly shone light on the local academy's examination fraud. Over a hundred mandarins were implicated in this, the highest of which was second-rank, all were eventually rounded up.

Upon becoming Deputy Minister, you handled the case of former Judicial Review Office Deputy Minister Yu Cheng forging evidence, causing him to be expelled from the Office's Haoran Pavilion, with all status stripped from him thereafter...
Are these case histories, as stated, true?
...Yes.
With an academic background like this, surely everyone lauds you as a young up-and-comer blessed to the gills with talent.
And yet people can't help but wonder—what gives such a greenhand mandarin the courage, the capacity to handle these cases?
Can you get to the point?
If I'm remembering right, your good mother and father were Tianshis at the Chamber of Heaven's Designs, spending years building much in the north, even being vested personally by the True Lung.
The Lin family's a well-bred one, many Tianshi giants in its line. And on top of that, rumor has it they're quite closely-knit with the Lord Exorcist's household.
As for Cyanstorm Count... I think even the rank fails to sufficiently capture your shifu's importance to Great Yan.
...Are these relationships, as stated, true?
Jie Zhen, what are you doing?
You need only answer 'yes' or 'no', Deputy Minister Lin.
Don't think I'm deaf to your connotations... You're trying to say, it was only by leaning on my background that I could take on Judicial cases all these years?!
...I certainly didn't say that at any point.
Having known each other for years, I would never doubt your integrity. The Lin Qingyan in my mind's eye has always been a pursuer of justice.
But why, Lin, do you not pursue now?
Jie Zhen... Just why did you come to see me today?
How much do you really know about the Yu Cheng affair?
As I just said, I'm here to see you today on routine business.
As for the Yu Cheng case, what I heard in the courtroom that day is all I know.
What makes you so dead-set on the case, Censor Jie Zhen?
Before you decided you'd investigate it, I remember we actually had an argument.
I'm going to speak out of line here... the whole stripping of his rank aside, I really do respect people like Yu Cheng.
Now of all times, you're going to defend him again?

If a law enforcer does not themselves abide by the law, that's abuse of power. That goes for every last member of the Three Organs of Justice—what kind of double-standard could you possibly be applying here?
No, no... The Judicial Review Office's verdict is settled on Yu Cheng's mistake. I've little more to say there.
I'm just very curious... What kind of truth could have pushed him to such means to find it?
You must be more familiar with Yu Cheng than me.
In your opinion, was he a smart man?
Naturally. When it comes to sniffing out lies or chasing down leads, I'm no match for him.
Then did you ever wonder if, supposing Yu Cheng truly intended to cover up his own forgery, you should have been able to make such short work of the case?
You're telling me he let me find him out on purpose? What for?
That, I don't know. But he put you in charge of the scrollery fire case... To an outsider, I don't think I can say that Yu Cheng had no faith in you.
You've been all over Baizao the past few days investigating that case, I know that. I've heard as much.
And yet since yesterday, you've been loitering in the Judicial Review Office, acting like you've been grounded.
I can't help being curious—what made you go and give up on pursuing this case?
Are you afraid of offending the high and mighty? No, that's not like the Lin Qingyan I know...
Did someone lofty crack down on you? Can't be, I think. You're sitting here in the office, after all, not in a Judicial prison cell.
If not that, then all my pondering can only produce one answer...
You're scared that if you continue, you'll implicate someone you know.
Seems I guessed right.
In the thick of things, your concern clouds your judgment. It's like you've forgotten the simplest of truths.
Don't miss the forest for the trees.
If you can't hide it, you may as well tip it out. Show it some sunlight.
Mr. Censor, you've really served up a lesson for me today.
Not at all. I think the hush of this office must've helped you figure out plenty by yourself.
I know what I must do.
Madam, I went to ask Deputy Minister Lin if she wanted anything to eat, since I saw she hadn't left the office all day, but—but she...
When did she go missing?
I—I don't know, nobody's seen her at all...
A biting chill gusts through, the stiff breeze curling papers and sending them dancing madly through the air.
A wide-open window clatters against its wall. On the table beneath it, a ceremonial gown lies neatly folded and topped with a document, pressed down in place by an ink stone.
It is Lin Qingyan's written resignation.
Watching as Blaze vanishes far into the distance, Ning Shu clutches the door knocker.
The vague dark point vanishes over the crest of the path, but her stare remains fixed there, lingering for the longest time.
What are you spacing out at the door for?
Grandfather...
Your friend left so soon? She won't even stay for dinner?
Mm... she had urgent business. She couldn't stay.
Even as a child, you never brought friends over. Here I thought I could drop in to see what she's like.
I don't know if we even—count as friends, really...
Seeing the dejected Ning Yin, the old man tries to think of something to say, but can't. He simply reaches over and brushes a leaf off her shoulder.
Almost time to move. Plenty of things still on your plate, eh?
What about you, Grandfather?
Heheh, for days I was all raring to go home. Now that we're about to, I look around at our little garden and I think 'oh, if only I could stay'.
Do you remember? It was here in this pavilion we laid out some sand, and grabbed a stick, and I taught you how to write one stroke at a time.
You said by next year, you'd be writing all the estate couplets for us. Then you spent two months with me learning, and after that you had to give up.
But by next year, you really did write me a couplet.
'Bountiful blooms and a full moon; may joy last forever.'
I'm surprised you still remember, Grandfather.
How could I forget... Ah, another year, another Mid-Autumn. How quickly the days pass.
The two of us haven't chewed the fat like this in the longest, have we? Grandfather to granddaughter.
Mm. I've been out constantly, and you're always occupied with guests. We hardly have the chance to meet.
The whole family's leaving the capital, after all. Quite the headache.
But things will settle down once we're back in Langyi, and we'll have plenty of time to chat along the way.
Are you almost done writing that book of yours?
Grandfather, I don't... think I can go back to Langyi with you.
When did you make up your mind?
Ning Yin shakes her head softly. She falls a few steps back.
You're returning home, Grandfather, like a leaf returns to its root. It's human nature, the whole family sympathizes. But the journey is meandering enough to make us worried.
The Nings are bursting with talent. Everyone's work forms a cornerstone of Yan. Compared to them, I really am the lone choice to accompany you back.
But frankly, I was always a distant orphan from Langyi anyway. It was because you took such pity on me that I could spend these decades at the Ning manor.
If I go now to Langyi, I go back to where my long-gone parents once lived.
That was your thinking... when you asked to accompany me home?
Grandfather... that was my thinking.
It all seemed to fall into place. I almost managed to convince myself.
But I've spent the last few days on a farewell tour of sorts around Baizao's streets, so I can finish up the Baizao Cuisine & Food Culture somehow.
And I realized, all my memories are of this city.
I can clearly recall what year we planted that persimmon tree in the courtyard, and when its fruits make for the best wine.
And every restaurant of every road and alley, and what their chefs are like, and how many different ways you can cook each dish.
All of a sudden, it hit me that... this very city, moreso than the Ning manor, feels like my home.
*sigh*...
...That's on me. That's on me for not asking you how you feel. I've been nothing but me this whole time.
Grandfather, you're my one and only family. You've always been since I was little.
I really did want to go back home with you at first, but the reason I'm set on staying in Baizao now... is you as well, Grandfather.
Is it?
Mm-hm.
When I first joined the Imperial Historical Cabinet to compile annals, there was one thing I could never puzzle out.

At some specific point in Yan's millennia of history, out of nowhere, the written word proliferated and education became for all. Even the earliest of the tablets we were studying only went back to that period.
So I thought, in that period, someone must have pushed for those changes. But I flipped through practically all the material we had, and I couldn't find a single clue.
I had this strange feeling that, there in Yan's history, one person seemed to be missing.
Then one day, I met someone strange at a Go club, who told me the story of 'that person'.
I thought he was just a yarn-spinning storyteller at first, but as I further researched, I gradually found all his stories to be corroborated.

According to him, 'she' was a gentle person who gave her heart and soul, her blood and sweat to the wellbeing of the people. And she gave too much... But how could someone as good as her fail to leave her name in our annals?
That was when I realized that a history book isn't a container, but a sieve. The stories that stay behind are few and far between.
Grandfather... I want to record your story. But then I look back, and it dawns on me that for all the years I've spent with you, I can't even write with any clarity about...
About what—kind of person you are.
Ning Yin finally lifts her head, and meets the old man's eyes.
She waits for his reply, his answer to her unspoken question.
She's waited for so long.
Who goes there!
Who goes where?
Tch... Thought I heard something.
It's all the bizarre cases these days. Makes me feel like someone's going to barge in any moment and wreak havoc.
This is the Judicial Review Office. What kind of miscreant would break in here?
Quit jumping at ghosts. Guard duty's almost over, and then we can clock out and head home.
Just wait a sec and then enter through the front... say you're here to report a case, then find a floor map on the way to the waiting room...
The window in the waiting room yesterday faces north, the Judicial entrance faces south, behind is a high perimeter wall.
Lin Qingyan's office is sec—third floor... get out the window, climb up the sprinkler pipes, and no one should notice me.
Ta ma de! Once I find her I'm gonna sock her in the...!
Who's there?!
Lin Qingyan?!
Blaze?!
What are you here for?!
What am I here for? I'm here to get YOU back!
Where'd your old court uniform go?
I resigned.
Then went back to the restaurant, couldn't find you, and worried you might be up to something stupid, so thank you for confirming that fear.
Now let's find somewhere else to talk!
You're telling me that all those years, Gu Quan was staying at the Ning manor?
But how did you find out...
Never you mind that. Now, can you tell me exactly what that means?
Minister Ning's involvement in the Gu Quan case runs deeper than I ever thought. He was clearly hiding something in his previous testimony.
I did suspect he was the perpetrator, but going by Mo Bufu's testimony, when Gu Quan was sent to death row in the fallout of the old case, Ning Shu was the one who got him out. Knowing that...
He wouldn't have a motive to try and get him killed again.
And the most key thing... I had no idea before that Yu Cheng had contact with Gu Quan so early on.
Whatever's connecting this string of events, it's likely more complicated than anything I've imagined.
See, I'm still curious. All those years ago, my dad came to Baizao on his own—and what the hell for?
Yes, that's the key question...

(Blaze has no clue about the two lives Gu Quan lived... If he was a fall guy in that incident, then why would he return to Baizao a dozen years after the fact and plant himself in the middle of the maelstrom?)
(Ning Shu's position, Yu Cheng's scheme... We still can't draw an outline around any of these questions.)
(How should I let Blaze know...)
Hey, you know I can tell that look on your face?
C'mon. What's our next move, exactly?
Blaze, about yesterday...
Forget about it. I know you 'it's-my-duty' types get the urge to do a little weird stuff now and then. Not my circus, I'm not arguing.
Just don't ever tell me any stupid crap like 'I'm sending you back for your safety' again, because—
You're still trying to ditch me!?!
Blaze, trust me, I will see this case through to the end... but you can't stick around in Baizao any longer than this.

Do as I said. Leave within two days, head to the Lungmen office first. But you can't stay there for too long either, so once you're reorganized, get back on the road straight away and leave Yan as soon as—
Over my dead body.
You don't trust me to keep investigating?
I mean, I do, but you're out of a rank, you're out of a job, how are you gonna investigate?
I'll think of something.
If Yu Cheng can resign and investigate in secret, then so can—
I have an idea.
Let's do this thing a little more thoroughly.
What's your plan?
Blaze pulls a letter from her pocket.
It's the chance Mo Bufu gave her, and now it's both of their chances.
Not like we're gonna get anywhere just running around town.
We'll head to the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies, go straight to the True Lung, and ask him to explain all this.
Your Imperial Majesty, the Ministry of Rites has drafted the official list of attendees for the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies.
To mark the occasion, Victoria, Leithanien, Laterano and other such nations have sent us their envoys with letters of celebration.
For Your Imperial Majesty's perusal.
*cough* *cough*... *cough* *hack*...
Your Imperial Majesty... the Ministry of War and Sui Regulator have finished drawing up joint battleplans.
Also for Your Imperial Majesty's... *cough* *cough*... perusal.
The Ministry of Revenue was not consulted on this battle's drain on labor and fiscal resources, nor on the postwar reparations. How were these battleplans drafted?

I know you and the Ministry of Revenue are both very busy. The Ministry of War simply went to some small pains working out the manpower, the funding, the ration, and even the Ministry of Works's postwar repair expenses.
And now you can read together with His Majesty, since we're having an audience and all.
Logistics are calculable, but how many of Yan's people will bleed in this war? Is that a bill you can work out so easily?
So the Grand Tutor means to say, if you can't work it out, then no need to try?
It's autumn... How are the harvests in the north?
Dahuang has resumed production ASAP following the disaster. Our harvest this year is more or less on par with previous years.
In that case... what about next year?
Your Imperial Majesty...
A life measured in years, yet the stress feels ancient. We're running out of time.
This involves the livelihoods of all Yan's people. I ask that Your Imperial Majesty reconsider.
Grand Tutor, the Ministries have long discussed everything worth discussing. It's about time we made up our minds.
His Majesty's decree is made. We simply await his proclamation at the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies.
It's growing late. It looks to rain.
...The two of you can leave.
May we be excused.
The man behind the curtain lifts his gaze—
In this desolate palace, three banquet seats lay set out in a row to his side, and one of them is truly showing its age.
Faintly, a sigh.
Aiyo, aiyo... Trust this rain to come from nowhere, I'm soaked...
Autumn drizzles and cold snaps... We'll be out of fresh vegetables in a few days at this rate. I didn't even get to rustle up this year's winter clothes...
Chef, I think we're forced to start our winter pickles now. Get your mess of stuff out of the storehouse as soon as—
Chef...?
Blaze? Xingzhu? Where'd everybody go...?
The ever-lively kitchen is presently deserted, all the cookware tidied away. The cramped space seems a good bit wider like this.
The only presence is a pot of soup boiling on the stove.
You sure are lucky.
All my years at the Judicial Review Office, and you are the first I have seen who has the chance to get out of this damn prison alive.
I myself never expected it to turn out this way.
Hmph. That's what's wrong with all you clerical types. Always living for the day, never thinking about the consequences.
All that time spent researching, all those years of blood, sweat and tears, all for a dish at the Feast and one very angry Lung.
You caused quite the ruckus and ended up in here. Are you happy?
It is as you say...
But my deeds were loyal and just. This, I know. My conscience is clear.
Those who peer into finponds to find omens will see omens... So, what comes next in your plans?
The order to transfer has already been issued. I will be joining the diplomatic mission to Victoria in three days.
After all that happened, I can no longer stay in Baizao. These arrangements are the fruit of Minister Ning's efforts.
Victoria? How are you supposed to get to Victoria with a body like yours?
To be favored with a pardon is all I need ask for.
Besides, I can consider going to Victoria returning home...
Can you really? I remember you saying your wife and child already passed away, no?
Yes... It has been many years since that day.
...Even if I did believe you, do you think other people will?
If you can remember things that happened back then, so can others.
Do you know how many eyes will be watching you when you return?
What am I to do...? Please, speak plainly.
Lend me your life, my friend.
That unvarnished truth you crave? I will give it to you.
The first time I came to Baizao to further my studies left a deep impression on me. Skyscrapers that blotted out the heavens, an unending sea of brilliant lights.
And in the center of all those lights was the Forbidden City.
A thousand feet it teetered, its lofty heights unseen from the ground.
My teacher said this was where every policy was decided, where every decree was handed down, each one affecting the lives of untold commoners. A duty heavier than mountains.
And thus it is here that every student can put to use all they have learned.
To serve the lands and the people.
But when I stood here, in the flesh, facing that palace of edifice and high walls—
I felt a chill sap away my breath.
You have arrived early, Mr. Liang.
Miss Ning...?
Hmm? You seem surprised. Do you not wish to see me?
Hardly.
I simply had not heard you would also be attending the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies, Miss Ning.
Minister Ning is ill, so for this year's Feast, the Ministry of Rites had no choice but to send me to attend in his stead.
It was a rather last-minute decision, so I did not have the time to tell you, my Lord.
I suppose that leaves us equals in silence, since it seems you never planned to tell me you were attending either.
I am merely here as part of my work. I did not wish to bother you, Miss Ning.
Hmm...
I am suddenly reminded of how, aside from Shangshu, we have not met for the better part of a year. Several of my letters to you during that time received no answer.
It can be hard to put words to paper, and I find myself often mired in official business. I have been negligent in maintaining my side of the conversation.
I cannot deny your blame, Miss Ning.
Where have I mentioned blaming you, my Lord?
During your time as magistrate, even the smallest needs of your hometown never left your thoughts. Now with expanded duties? The concerns have only increased.
But for some reason, after only six short months, I feel as though we have become estranged.
As if there are some things... we cannot say to the other.
Putting that aside for the moment, this is the first time you are attending the Feast, yes?
Yes. My early years were spent in Shangshu, so I had little opportunity to take part in government events such as this.
Major topics are often deliberated upon during these Feasts.
The question this year is, what decision will His Majesty make in regards to this war?
I dare not make any sort of wild conjecture on His Majesty's thoughts.
But you are a clever man, Mr. Liang. Your conjectures are always correct.
You did in fact have something on your mind the other day, and you wanted to discuss it with Grandfather, did you not?
I dare not lie to you, Miss Ning.
Phrase it how you will. I am always happy to hear that you wish to see me.
But I am afraid that for you, I have never been someone to be trusted, or to entrust things with.
I believe there may have been a misunderstanding, Miss Ning...
I do not mean to blame you.
Who among us did not detest the murky secrets of the bureaucracy when these red robes were first draped over us? But now, we must navigate all this wheeling and dealing, like it or not.
I may not be close to the center of the court, but there is one thing even I know.
It is difficult to speak the truth, and even more difficult to make others believe you are speaking the truth.
Once you enter the palace walls, the word 'sincerity' means less than nothing.
But all I have ever shown you is sincerity, Miss Ning.
No matter what may come, I have always hoped you would remember—
We should take our seats now, Mr. Liang. Let us head inside.
Come on, step on it! Check over your ingredients one last time when you're done! The guests are already seated! We need to start serving as soon as the order comes in!
Inspect ALL your ingredients! Check on the stews and soups while you're at it!
The Bliss Soup on stove number ninety... I don't know how, but the color seems to be a bit off.

What?! How many times did I say to cut the ingredients into finger-width cubes! No more, no less! And it's supposed to be on the fire for twenty hours! No breaks! Someone must have screwed something up!
*sigh*...! Alright, move aside, I'll come have a look!
(Quietly) Hey... Over here!
Why're you still wearing those clothes?! Scared people won't notice you or something?
Formal dress for formal occasions. I had kept these clothes at home just in case.
But this is the kitchen! Don't you have anything more... suitable to put on?
No.

You—
Hey!
Is disguising yourself not something natural for Judicial Officers?
What nonsense are you talking about? Deputy Minister at the Judicial Review Office is not some undercover ghost—
Though I have found myself sneaking around a lot lately for some reason...
Alright, enough muttering, change into these clothes. You follow the army of waiters into the banquet area as they're serving up the dishes, then change back into your fancy robe when you can.
Fine...
Hah... This kind of infiltration really does make me a little nervous...
You said you were going to stand up to see justice restored, so if you make the True Lung mad, is he just going to arrest you right away, or is he going to...

There is a tradition of remonstration during the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies. Besides, we're just going to request a reopening of an old case due to inconclusive evidence. Why would the True Lung be mad at me?
Although there is one thing...
Which is?
Have you ever thought about... What if...
What if the truth we manage to dig up on this case turned out to be the complete opposite of what we thought...
What if... you found out everything around you was one big lie...
What are you talking about?
Didn't we do all this to get to the truth in the first place? If it's a lie, we'll smash it to pieces! Why the hesitation?!
We've come this far already. What choice do we even have?
Okay.
Blaze... I think... Your father, he... He was likely a good man.
Huh? Why this all of a sudden?
Promise me this. If something unexpected happens, you must take care of yourself fir—
Shh! Someone's coming, hide! Under the stove!
Is everything ready?
R—Ready, chef.
These longevity noodles you are in charge of may be simple, but they are still vital for the feast. Make no mistakes while preparing them.
U—Understood.
You...
Although I do not know why your shifu did not wish to come to the Feast, I believe he had his reasons to choose you.
Once the Feast is over, I will give you the opportunity to take on my mantle.
Eh? Me?
Not happy to hear that?
Hahaha... There's a lot of chefs who would pinch themselves awake if they were told they were going to be your apprentice.
But I don't understand. Why're you entrusting all the skills you've learned your entire life to only one person?
Nonsense... Do you know just how many examinations a Ding Feng Lou head chef puts their prospective students through before they choose an apprentice?
Not to mention the fact that my own shifu was the one who penned the 'Book of Foods' with recipes from all four major cuisines and has served us well for decades worth of Feasts.
Out of the hundreds of chefs in this Ding Feng Lou kitchen who have honed their craft for many years, Shifu chose me to take the reins. Just me.
Generations of exquisite artisanship in these hands, you'd let anybody smudge it?
But, no matter how exquisite the artisanship is, you're making the dish for people to eat...
...Hmmm?
Haha... What I mean is, is teaching a single apprentice who's as good as you really more important than teaching a bunch of chefs who can do a decent job...?
The more you teach, the more can cook, which means even more of Yan can enjoy tastier food, or something...
...That's enough of your disrespectful rubbish!
Focus on your noodles.
Have the preparations been made?

Yumen has entered the highest state of alert, and the city defenses are all in position. The citizens have been evacuated, leaving twenty thousand soldiers and three thousand Tianshis able to take to the field at any time.
They await your orders.
Okay.
Let us see if the moons wax or wane in our favor tonight.
General, the Grand Commandant's Messenger has arrived.
At this hour?
Where is the message, then?
He didn't have one. All he had to deliver was... a box of mooncakes.
He said the Grand Commandant had only one thing to say to you, General.
'Eat them while they're hot. Don't let them get cold.'
You received my notice, Lord Zuo.
It has been a while... Are you faring well?
You...
No need to be so tense. This pawnshop has long since closed its doors. No-one will be bothering us.
Why meet me alone?
Shouldn't I be asking you that, Lord Zuo?
What do you mean?
I opened this pawnshop on a whim during my stay in Baizao, and for a time, business was booming.

Every day would see customers bring in all sorts of curios, scrolls, precious baubles and odd gemstones... In the end, many of them were taken by my wandering younger brother, and placed within his garden.
The most curious thing was that all the customers who came by this shop had one thing in common: they were looking for me.
You think far too highly of yourself.
Well, since you have come to see me, does that not mean you have something to discuss, Lord Zuo? Perhaps I could answer one or two questions plaguing you?
All I want to know is... Why have you come to Baizao?
If that convict has decided now is the time to declare war on Yan, all that awaits him is death and destruction... But I'm sure he knows that already.
Indeed. My elder brother is skilled at the art of the gridded board and naught else, though it is a formidable and well-honed skill at that.
Do you still remember the Shanhaizhong who appeared in Yumen, Lord Zuo?

During the Sui Regulator's analysis of the incident, we noticed traces of another Feranmut proxy's actions.
Wang is suspected of being involved...
What are you trying to say?
I'm sure you remember, Lord Zuo, that during that hunt a thousand years ago, the Feranmut of Yan were either put to the sword, or banished.
Has the Sui Regulator ever wondered, considering my brother was able to find one of them...
Could he, perhaps, have found more than just one?

During the many years since the incident, the Sui Regulator have watched closely his every movement within Yan's borders. But have they never considered the possibility that one of his weiqi stones might leave the country?

Don't underestimate our intelligence network. The Sui Regulator have never stopped keeping an eye on the Feranmut outside. If they make any sudden movements, our Feranmutologists will surely sense something.
I'm sure they did. They should be on their way back to Yan right about now.
Please treat this information as my humble gift to you, Lord Zuo. How you use it is for you to decide.
Why are you telling me this? You're one of the convict's conspirators, so why tell us his plans?
In truth... Though I am indeed in collaboration with my brother, our hearts are not as one. We have our differences.
He insists on seeing a victor decided. I, on the other hand, prefer doing my business in a more... amicable fashion.
As for why I chose you, Lord Zuo, I feel like we are... kindred spirits.
What?
I profess I have some skill in reading people. My intuition tells me we will have the opportunity to do business once again in the future.
It is possible that the fate of us siblings will lie in your hands, Lord Zuo.
To be able to chat with you on this festive day, even but for a moment, is destiny at work... I hope that in the future, you can see me as a 'man' you can discuss things with.
Aiyo!
What's wrong?!
Sorry! M—My hand just cramped! Couldn't hold it steady!
You seriously... Bah!
Sorry! I'll go back to the kitchen and get a new one!
Do it quick! The mandarins are all seated already. Once the True Lung arrives, the banquet will start!
Do you need help, madam?
Madam?
*cough*... I am here for the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies.
All the mandarins should have already been seated. May I ask why...
Forgive me, I was delayed on the road here. I will head in right away.
Please wait a moment.
Madam, please show your invitation to the Feast.
Why? Is me standing here not invitation enough?
A routine inspection. I beg your pardon, madam.
My Lady! You dropped something of yours!
You...?
You left in such a rush! How could you not have noticed you dropped something so important? Good thing I spotted it.
Ah, Madam Tang of the Ministry of Revenue. Please forgive my earlier impertinence.
There are no further issues here, but the Feast is about to begin. Please take your seat as quickly as you can.
That invitation is a forgery. It will only work the one time. Do take care when you're inside.
I know you... You're one of the ladies at the Ning Manor.
I'm also a friend of Blaze.
I spotted her name on the chef roster and guessed at your plan.
The rest, you will simply have to beg forgiveness from the True Lung after meeting him.
But don't you know... If this case is reopened, you'll be ruined.
But the one most heavily implicated by this case would be Blaze, no?
Didn't you already agree to help her find the truth? What else is there to worry about?
Besides... If you really do manage to pull back the curtain on all this, well, that's what I was after in the first place.
Thank you...
Despite all my years as a mandarin, I rarely set foot in the Forbidden City.
I see towering palaces press up against the sky, majestic and stately.
I see a banquet of incomparable bounty, each and every seat filled. The lamps are as bright as day, even as the moonlight envelops them.
But what I cannot see...
Is who hid away these names. Who does not want to see the truth come to light?
I suddenly recall the words.

lines.st: 'Will you be able to handle the truth?'

lines.x: Yu Cheng... What exactly did you see...?
How strange. The Feast has already started, so why has His Imperial Majesty not yet arrived?
No need to panic. His Imperial Majesty is busy. Something urgent might have come up. All we can do is wait.
I heard he's been ill for a while now. What if it is something to do with that?
Shh! Stop blabbering.
Preposterous! Simply preposterous!
Don't you know where we are!? What right have you to obstruct a court mandarin?
What's all this noise about?
This man says he's Assistant Minister Tang Jian of the Ministry of Revenue, but we've already checked, and every seat under the Ministry of Revenue is already accounted for...
I AM TANG JIAN! None of this 'he says' rubbish! Look, do you see this seal in my hand? Whose name is on it?!
...Hmmm?
All falls silent.
Three blasts of the horn mark the seventh hour of the night.
The moons shine bright overhead, their light so clear they wash away all color.
All rise—
One by one, the mandarins stand, looking towards the center of the Feast with bated breath.
Waiting for the one meant to be seated at the head of the table—
I have something to report, Your Majesty!
Fire.
None knew where the burst of flame came from, but all it needed was a gust of autumn wind to engulf the entire banquet.
The flickering flames act like wild beasts, brandishing their fangs and claws as they pace about above the table.
The wind blows, and the beasts of fire grow and grow and grow with speed. The conflagration bloats and bulges, letting off unsettling noises. Some liken it to shouting, others to laughter—
Bizarre flames pour like falling rain. Having already tainted the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies, they flit and flicker as they climb up the faces of the panicking mandarins.
What the... What's going on?
Fire! FIRE!
Assassins! Protect His Majesty!
Guards, guards—
Put out that fire! Lock the dining area down!
Take care—
The source of this fire...
Everyone! Stay away from the wine in the cups!
(A fire that does not burn hot...)
(Who did this... and what are they up to?)
Lin Qingyan tries to calm down the panicking crowd, but her voice is quickly drowned out by the shouting and screaming.
In the midst of the chaos, she spots a familiar face.
Their eyes met for only a moment, but she is more sure than anything.
That person saw her, saw her attempt to cross the line of propriety in front of all.
But the target is not her.
Blaze...!
What's going on? What's with all this ruckus?
This is bad! I heard some kind of demonic fire broke out at the banquet! No idea how it happened!
The guards have the dining area surrounded. No idea what's going on inside, or if His Majesty is hurt...
They're talking about... cutting the Feast short...
Lin Qingyan...!
We've been notified of unknown persons infiltrating the Feast. We will now be conducting an inspection.
I ask that everyone stay where they are and not move. Please cooperate.
(Dammit... What the hell happened over where Leizi is?)
(This is bad. I can't leave her to fend for herself. I need to go take a look...)
You there! Stop moving!
(What do I do what do I do? I can't get in a fight here!)
(No time to think, dammit!)
After her!
First Battalion, Fifth Battalion, seal off the southern entrance! Seventh and Ninth, close the northern gate!
Everyone else, sweep the palace from south to north. If you find anyone suspicious, arrest them immediately.
Our intel says there are at least two culprits working together to ruin the Feast, so take care!
So many guards... What a pain.
What kinda trouble did you manage to get yourself into here, Leizi?
Sounds like you haven't gotten caught yet, at least... So where the hell are you?
Tsch... This palace is way too big...
Why're you here?!
Shh—
I thought you two just wanted to ask His Majesty to reopen the case? How did it come to this?
I don't know either! That fire's not our doing!
But the timing...
You need to leave, right away! There's no talking your way out of it if they find you.
What I need to do is save Lin Qingyan!
...Let me go look for her.
How can I ask you to do something that dangerous?!
I'm more familiar with the Forbidden City than you are. Even If I did get caught, I'll have some leeway to explain myself.
Don't worry. I'll find her.
But...
You still don't trust me. What, still angry at me?
Of course not—
Head to the southern exit, before the guards seal it off!
We'll meet up at that restaurant once we're all out of here.
...Okay.
You take care too.
Wait, did the soldiers manage to get a good look at you?
Err... Yeah, I think so.
That's... not good.
Oh, I know! Let's swap clothes. Come on, get them off.
I saw someone running away up ahead! Over there!
*Sigh*...
The dining area's been cleared out... Deputy Minister Lin wouldn't be here.
But who started all this at the Feast...
I hope Blaze managed to make it out by now—
Why is she here?
These two...
Is this fate at work?
Looks like it's not too far away now...
Wonder how things are going over on their side...
Found you...
Who's there?!
So it was you.
Damn... That's one hell of a sword!
You're not dressed like your average soldier. What are you, a general?
Save the chatter.
So you're the one Lin Qingyan has been hiding behind her back... But why would she bring you to the Forbidden City?
Err... So I know it's impolite to barge in on someone else's banquet, but would you believe me if I said we didn't mean anything bad by it...?
What I'm trying to say is that this is all a misunderstanding...
There is no misunderstanding here. The Imperial Guard have been searching for you for forty years.
You are her descendant.
What are you talking about? Whose descendant? Who's 'her'?

You really don't know—
No. It seems you're not lying.
All this time you've been hidden away, and nobody thought to tell you about your history?
Haha, got me there... I'm pretty curious about it, not gonna lie. Anyone I can ask about it?
But seriously, the Imperial Guard? Looking for me?
Forty years ago, only a single child was unaccounted for in the family of a treasonous mandarin.
You... What are you talking about?
Blaze... I need to confirm something...
You were born in 1062, in Victoria, right?
Yes. From as far back as I can remember, I lived in Victoria.
Your father seemed to have been spending a lot of time pursuing a certain 'truth' in the last few years of his life.
What if... you found out everything around you was one big lie...
Wha...?
Y—You're talking nonsense! Bi zui!
You are a person of interest. We'll figure out what to do with you after questioning.
Come with me.
Forget that!
(Gah—!)
You're no weakling, it seems. I might not be able to take you in without hurting you.
If you don't wish to be harmed, then surrender immediately.
I'm not in the mood for jokes right now!
(Panting heavily)
Didn't I tell you... to save yourself if... something happened...?
...Lin Qingyan.
Lin Qingyan's eyes meet Blaze's.
She understands what has happened, but all she has to offer is silence.
She turns and stands in front of Blaze, shielding her.
Your deeds at the Judicial Review Office are worthy of my respect, Deputy Minister Lin. And I was kind enough to remind you before.
It seems you have decided to completely ignore my warning.
I am perfectly aware what I'm doing. I don't need your reminders.
You know who she really is. Yet to go so far as to protect the descendant of a traitorous mandarin...
Why?
For the truth. The truth you are all too afraid to talk about.
Lin Qingyan!
What are you two talking about...? What do you mean... I'm the descendant of a traitor?
My dad... Who is he? Why did he die...?
Forgive me. I was planning to tell you everything after tonight. But it seems now is not a good time.
I will make it up to you later however you like. Provided we make it out of here alive, that is.
She will be coming with me tonight.
As long as I am here, she will not.
Forget that. You will need three of you to simply obstruct me.
Lin Qingyan!
Go already!
A burst of lightning blasts open a hole in the palace wall. Before the dust has even settled, a somewhat gentler blast of energy strikes Blaze.
She is sent flying out of the Forbidden City, falling towards a distant patch of ground.
Blaze has done these sort of high-altitude leaps countless times before as a form of dynamic entry into the battlefield, or wherever happened to need her.
She has never once felt fear. She believed in her own strength, and in her many friends.
There was never any confusion in her mind either. She knew who she was. She knew where she must go. She knew what she believed in.
There is no plot that can hold back her heat, just like how the mist of dawn cannot hide away the sun.
But now?
What awaits her is no longer solid ground, but an endless abyss.
She closes her eyes.
Where...?
Ning Yin lies on the bed, her breathing faint.
The crutch in the old man's hand shudders, and he nearly falls over.
After having seen so many things across so many years, he thought nothing more in this world could shock him.
His lips open and close, yet try as he might, he can make no sound.
Until someone in the shadows begins to speak first.
She yet lives.
It's you—
Stay your steps, Mr. Ning. Come no closer.
You never saw me tonight, and she never left the house.
She... But why...?
She intruded upon the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies. She was with that girl when I discovered her.
If she had run into anyone else, she would not still be alive.
Impossible...
Forty years... I can't believe it's you again...
Forty years ago, you left her here. Now, you save her life once more, save her from a terrible fate.
Fate... This must be fate...
There's no escaping it. There never was.
I did not leave her here for her sake. It was you who saved her, Mr. Ning.
You once swore an oath to never let anyone know the truth of this case in exchange for her name. But it seems you are now wavering when it comes to past vows.
The True Lung will know of tonight's events.
The choice is yours, Mr. Ning.
A gust of wind, and it was like nobody was even there.
The old man stands in stunned silence.
His daze is interrupted as he hears a sound coming from outside the courtyard walls.
A sound like roaring, like weeping, like admonishment.
I must be going mad...
It's autumn. Why do I hear cicadas...?
Ah—!
Where... How did I get here?!
Oh, finally awake I see.
I knew you were one tough Feline. Look at you, as lively as a fin on a line.
Jiang was making a fuss about calling a doctor. He was shouting so hard he forgot what time it was.
How long was I out... Wait, where are they?!
Lin Qingyan... Did Ning Yin come by here? I... Seriously, how did I get here?
Alright, slow down. I don't even know where to start with all these questions.
Last night I was on my way to the night market to get some cheap veggies when I found you lying down in the middle of the street. Thought you were drunk at first.
I was worried you'd freeze to death or something, so I brought you back here.
You were cursing up a storm in your sleep, hypocrite this, swindler that, and a lot more besides... Tsk, I don't know where you managed to learn such creative Yanese.
The Ning Yin you mentioned, and whoever this Lin Qingyan is... Never seen them before, so I dunno if they were here.
I need to go find them—
Whoa, whoa, where're you going?
The Forbidden City.
I just saw a whole bunch of guards patrolling around there. You think you can still get in?
I don't care. I have to go—
Who d'you think you're going to find, after having lost yourself like that?
You... What are you talking about?
Just speaking the truth here. You lost yourself, yeah?
You don't know where your self has gone, where it's wandered off to. You hear a few words from someone else and wouldn't you know it, you barely even know your own name, much less who you are.
Such absurdity...
A tangerine seed is blown from Jiangnan to Jiangbei, where it takes root and yields fine fruit. What let this seed grow so well wasn't Jiangnan or Jiangbei, but decades worth of rain and sun.
It's ultimately the same fruit. Jiangnan and Jiangbei might not be the same place, but an orange is an orange. Doesn't matter where you eat it.
All things begin, all things end. There is the outer truth, and the inner truth. Once you figure that out, then you can get back to asking your questions.
You... Do you know something about all this? What do you know?! Tell me!

What do I know? I'm a chef, all I know is how to season my ingredients, how to boil stuff, stir-fry stuff, regular fry stuff—
Oh, right. Seeing you reminded me of something.
Weren't you trying to find authentic tangerine cakes?
I think I know how to make those.
One tangerine cake... to go, please.
Oh, you're here.
Sending it to Victoria this time as well?

No... Not sending it. I need to go back right away... Though it's hard to say if I even can go back.
This time—
*cough* *cough* *cough*! Just a snack for the road.
Wow, your cough's gotten pretty bad.
I don't think you should be eating sweets right now. I'll boil you up a bowl of loquat soup.
I'm fine, I'm fine... It's not to do with my lungs.
Doesn't matter where the problem is, you've got to take care of yourself. You seen a doctor about it?
Yes, yes. I don't have much time left, apparently.
I'd say I'm beyond having to mind what I eat.
...Huh?
It's fine. Everyone has to die someday.
I've done pretty much everything I needed to get done—
How can you say something like that?!
Didn't you... Didn't you say you still have family in Victoria? They're waiting for you to go home.

Yes... I held onto hope that I could see them again, but it seems
*cough*... that won't be happening...
I might've been a useless good-for-nothing my entire life, but at least I can say I haven't disgraced what little ideals and righteousness I still have in me.
But only now that I'm at the end have I realized I owe them far, far too much...
*sigh* Isn't that just how people are? Things like that happen from time to time. If you try and be the perfect people pleaser, you're a fool.
But surely you still want to go home, right?
I want to see them, but if I don't, they'll have easier lives.
That's just how people are... If you want to get something done, you need to put something down.
Everyone else has put their things down, so I should too...
I have been in your care for so many years now, Young Chef. Even my final few words in this reverie of mine are for your ears only.
Then...
Is there still... something you want?
I want...
You need to pay more attention. Look at this spot you swept, it's still covered in ash.
Make sure not to miss any corners either. Anything dirty has to be cleaned. We are moving out soon, and we cannot leave behind any trouble for the house's next owner.
It is rather late, Mr. Ning. Where are you headed off to?
Oh, don't mind me, just going out for a walk.
Mr. Ning's been acting really odd today.
Why's he spending his Mid-Autumn Festival watching us clean the house instead of gazing at the moon?
That's enough questions out of you. Focus on your sweeping.
With such brilliant moonlight tonight, we should give it a pristine spot to land on.
How did I... get here...
Grandfather's study?
Her mind lurches on the edge of consciousness. She feels as though she has forgotten something important.
An old book lies upon the desk in silence.
Annals of Yan, Volume 124, Supplement 17... 1062... Since when was there such a book in the Imperial Historical Records?
...Grandfather?
It's been years since there was a criminal thrown into the fifth floor of this prison. What's the story tonight?
Assassinating someone at court. The Imperial Guard themselves are dragging the culprit here.
What a ruthless—!
Shh!
Don't think too hard about it, and don't ask too many questions. Just keep a close eye on the culprit. You and I won't be enough to fix any mistakes.
It seems that everything I've run into since my return to Baizao has been part of some grand plan.
A trail of clues laid out in front of me like bait, leading towards a dead end.
Not only was the perpetrator highly skilled in kung fu, but they also had intimate knowledge of the Judicial Review Office's layout. They must have been working with Yu Cheng for quite some time.
Yu Cheng asked you to take notes during his trial. This is why you stand here now, and it is also the reason I cannot allow you to participate any further.
Whatever Yu Cheng's plan, you are most certainly a part of it.
Deputy Minister Lin, surely you wouldn't ask if you didn't know the answer already?
Can you not see the truth, or do you simply pretend you can't because you don't dare to?
What makes you so dead-set on the case, Censor Jie Zhen?
As the moons slowly sink into the west, their light passes through the barred window and scatters on the far wall of the cell. The night air condenses into dew, beading on Lin Qingyan's brow.
Were it not for the scent of mold, this prison would actually be a good place to meditate.
Indeed. While I was waiting here, I managed to figure out many things that have been on my mind.
It was you who set the scrollery on fire.
Was it an answer I gave you here, or one you puzzled out beforehand?
We meet again, Deputy Minister Lin.
Hey, I'm not in the mood to discuss snacks with you right now. I've got people to save!
Calm down, take it easy. Is it any use being so high-strung when you have so much to do?
What do you know—
All I know is that if the heat ain't right, don't take off the lid.
*sigh* You can't skip out on meals if you have something big to do. If you don't eat, you're not getting anything done.
Here, try this. Is this the tangerine cake you're looking for?
You... Weren't you always saying I wouldn't be able to find it, even if I searched all of Baizao?
I also said I'm the best chef in all of Baizao. As long as the taste exists, I can replicate it.
And this one's my own creation. It won't count for our bet.
Isn't this the same tangerine cake you made before—
Despite her skepticism, Blaze shoves a cake into her mouth.
She has tried many fine dishes over the last few days, and learned a lot about how to critique food during that time. The cake in front of her cannot be considered 'tasty'.
Xingzhu was right. Too much sugar will overwhelm the fragrance of the tangerine peel, while too much oil destroys the texture of the cake itself.
It is not a tasty snack...
But it is an exact match for the taste in her memories.
How do you...
How do you know this recipe?
You were so hung up on my tangerine cake not being sweet enough. It got me wondering what kind of chef would make them that sweet.
Then I thought, what if the one who made them so sweet didn't do it for the taste?
That means...
You said you ate these in Victoria, right?
I'm reminded of a regular customer I had many years ago. He always ordered some tangerine cakes, and said he was going to send some off to his daughter in a foreign land.
I told him they'd spoil long before they got to her.
But he refused to believe me. He'd process it a bit himself, and wrap it up in layer after layer of letter paper. You could barely tell if it was a letter or some food by the end of it.
You knew him!
He came here... I've been searching for him all this time...
Do you... know what happened to him...?
I wouldn't say I knew him. He was just a regular customer of many years.
I vaguely remember him always coming over before it was even bright out to order the cheapest breakfast I had on the menu, then he'd sit by the window, like he was waiting for someone.
Waiting for someone? Who...?
How should I know? See for yourself.
Day breaks.
The faint full moons brush against the horizon. As the night come to a close, there are perhaps many in slumber, enjoying dreams of happy reunions.
Diligent hawkers are up early setting up their stalls, their shouts echoing down the streets, rousing the sleepers of the metropolis.
Blaze spots a familiar figure by the street corner.
This small restaurant and that serene manor are separated by no more than a street corner. A fact barely worth noticing during normal times.
Was this what he was looking at, year after year?
It's you...
It's really you...
You're here.
I never thought I would see you again in my lifetime.
The man leaning against the corner is clothed in near-rags. He grasps a mooncake in his hands, his face awash in crumbs, ignorant of his guest.
A smear of light leaks in through the bars of his cell window, painting the criminal's gaunt face in stark relief.
The Judicial Review Office is truly kind. Even us prisoners get a mooncake for the Mid-Autumn Festival.
It's delicious. Want a bite?
No need. I will be having my meals in this prison soon enough. I will have my own to enjoy.
Pity, we still have so much to talk about. It's not quite the same without something to munch on, right?
The old man lets out a dry laugh, taking a seat across from the shabbily-dressed prisoner.
For decades, you have wanted to ask an old man like me your questions. I suppose today is your lucky day?
You should hate me, Mr. Ning.
You were one step away from being able to go back home without a heavy heart, one step away from enjoying your twilight years with your family.
Just one small step... Hahahahaha.
I really should hate you, when you put it like that. I would like nothing more than to charge at you and tear at your flesh with my teeth.
But on second thought, you have given up decades of your life and your position as Deputy Minister at the Judicial Review Office for me, spending the rest of your years within this cell.
No matter how you look at it, it should be you who hates me.
Hahahaha... You're overthinking it, Mr. Ning.
I said it before, but I was never any good at my job, or at being a decent human being... Not like Gu Quan.

I'm a glutton, you see, the type that loves sniffing out deep, hidden truths. I tore open shells to get at the flesh, and got addicted to the sweet, sweet taste of marrow. I'll do anything for it, even if it leaves my hands bloodied and my teeth shattered.
During all these years, you were the biggest, plumpest shellbeast on my dining table. How could I ever hate you?
Even if I were to hate... It wouldn't be for anything on my side.
Your teeth are still in fine shape. I never saw them chip while they were gnawing on my old bones.
But Yu Cheng, why were you so sure I would have come to see you today?
If I had just walked away from all this, all your plots and plans would have gone to waste, no?
Hahahaha... Because I understand you. I understand you far too well, Mr. Ning.
I simply had to gamble, that during the years you spent bobbing around the hierarchy of mandarins, you would dirty your hands, but still retain your humanity.
You let Gu Quan live on while he was right under your nose, after all, and raised his daughter to boot.
You care about the fate of that child... Those two children, rather. You wanted to see those girls live carefree, innocent lives while you yet live...
You knew that if you did not relent one day, they would never know peace.
And so you came to see me... You had to.
You have looked into these matters already... It seems you managed to find out most of what should have been found out.
How long did it take you?
I only finished deciphering the clues and piecing them all together last year. It shames me to admit as a deputy minister, but my pace was a bit too slow.
Gu Quan, on the other hand? He managed to figure it all out years ago.
I never considered that...
The year he returned to Baizao, you managed to put him under your watch. But you never thought he would copy decades worth of letters from your home.

By peering through all those letters between grand mandarins and nobles, he, a ninth-rank ceremonial officer, found every survivor of that murder case. He unraveled the threads and pieced the truth together.
Gu Quan's diligence knew no bounds. I underestimated him.
Anything more you wish to say, Mr. Ning?
Hahahaha... What is there left to say? What sins must be admitted, I shall admit.
I really did send someone to kill the Minister of Justice who presided over the Grand Preceptor's case.
But not because he accused me of being the Grand Preceptor's collaborator in treason...
Rather, because the Grand Preceptor's treason was a miscarriage of justice all along.
Indulge me in my curiosity, Deputy Minister Lin. When did you figure it out?
I suspected it was you who set Ding Feng Lou alight.
Why? What would I have to do with that incident?
Call it a hunch.
Though you might not have used your kung fu at the time, a mask was not enough to prevent me from thinking that person's figure and skills were familiar. Very familiar.
Afterwards, at the Judicial Review Office, you sought me out and managed to cement this particular suspicion in my mind.

Come to think of it, you must have been in close contact with Yu Cheng as early as when I was preparing to impeach him. I never considered the possibility you were already cooperating with him at the time.
From the scrollery fire to the hanging scroll in the Ning Manor, and then on to the fire at Ding Feng Lou. This recent string of mysteries was you trying to lead me along a trail of clues, wasn't it?
That was rather rushed and sloppy, now that I look back.
And the fire at the Feast of a Hundred Delicacies...?
I must regret to inform you that was not my doing, truly. Merely an unexpected turn that happened to align with our goal.
Why did you two do all this?
Ridiculous as it may sound, this all started with an old case. Not a large one, yet not small either.
A case I happened upon by chance after I joined the Department of Discipline and Supervision.
The Minister of Justice, assassinated by a beggar in the middle of the street. A case I could barely believe, yet one hastily closed without meaningful buildup or a satisfying conclusion.
Though the assassin had been executed, his identity, his motive... There were simply too many vague unknowns in the file.
As I dug deeper, I noticed that every thread led towards an even bigger case, a case that had snipped all those threads short.
The Grand Preceptor's treason...
You've investigated that case too.

The betrayal of the three Grand Ministers, a case large enough to shake the court, and one that implicates countless others. Forget finding the truth, even just mentioning the Grand Preceptor's name at the time was the greatest of taboos.
'The Grand Preceptor incited the prince to regicide.' This was all the history books had to say on the matter.
But nobody knows the truth of what happened the night the old True Lung passed away.
Who was it who mobilized the city guard? Who went into the palace?
Only later did I realize I was not the only one looking into this case.
Was this how you got to know Yu Cheng?
Should I rather call it fowlbeasts of a feather, or friendship at first sight?
You said the two of you weren't able to follow the trail, so how did you determine the Grand Preceptor was wrongly accused?
It wasn't our research that lead to this conclusion.
The one who brought everything to light was Gu Quan.
Why?
Do you seriously not know why?
Of course I do. I just wanted to ask you how this 'why' tasted when you said it aloud.
...The people cry out, their hearts uneasy...
Only those who have experienced the struggle know that Yan's current peace and prosperity were hard-won.
It was a very, very long time ago.

The old True Lung was diligent. At the time, Originium industries were beginning to change the structure of this great land. Even during Yan's turbulent times, he did not allow her to fall behind the other nations of Terra.
And so it was that a great hero was infected with Oripathy in his twilight years. Believing his days were numbered, his many policies began to stray into bias and imbalance.
At the time, the prince wished to stop the True Lung... and the Grand Preceptor stood by his side.
This supposed support was her urging the prince to seize the throne?
The old True Lung died the night the prince entered the palace to admonish him. As for the prince? His whereabouts since that night are unknown. Such a tumultuous event demanded an explanation.
...Which required someone to be charged with everything.
Do you think the current True Lung was unaware of all this...?
Hmph.
Changes in the court naturally lead to unrest among the masses. 'The Purge of Traitors'. A very fitting slogan.

The then-young True Lung acted with an iron fist to sweep away many courtiers with evil intent, stabilizing the situation as quickly as possible. This ensured this spell of court intrigue never made it outside the walls of the Forbidden City.
For forty years, I assumed I was the only one aside from the True Lung himself to still remember what transpired.
Until ten or so years after that case ended, that young man stood in front of me once again.
I fear you speak of that time too lightly, Mr. Ning.
It was not only traitors who were 'swept away'. There was also the entirety of the Grand Preceptor's family, twenty-eight in all.

They were all sent to prison. Some committed suicide in their cells to prove their innocence. Those who lived were exiled. I tried to find some trace of them, but it was no use. Couldn't find a single one.
Except for a child... Spirited away during the turmoil, still swaddled in a blanket.
I never considered the possibility she still lived, yet never truly believed she had died.
It seems you've met her.
A few days ago. Her appearance is... not too similar. But their personality? It's like two peas in a pod.
